title: I need help to progress with Activity 3
source_category: Week 4 / 4.2 Visibility, get/set Methods and Constructors with Greenfoot

children.body:
I don't know how to start with activity 3. 
How do you change the initial program to specify that key UP, only moves car1?
I'm a newcomer on OOP but I undestand that car1 and car2 are two objects of the same class Car. So, I don't really see your solution. Does it work?

I have two issues with your proposal:
- How/When do you switch the value of gamemode from true to false or vice versa?
- You say: "if it's true, move "up", else move "w". But how do you do to make that "Up" only aplies to car1 and "w" only to car2?

Yeah it works I tried, there is a simple solution of Activity 3 of making sub class copy the whole coding  from the car from top to bottom, then change the keys to w,s,a,d ect in the new subclass. It will work.

I followed your approach: having car1 and car2 as two subclasses of Actor class.
It works!!! Now I have the two cars competing in a race 
However I am not sure this is an "elegant" solution :)
I think This is an elegant solution :) .

Hello RicardoMF,
I don't know how to modify move method since this method is defined in actor class and I only see the API to use this methods but I don't know how to modify them.
Thanks for your kind help. I got it.

My approach is similar to that of RicardoMF, but instead of adding a new constructor I introduced a new method setControls(...), which then allows you to set custom controls like this: car2.setControls("w", "s", "a", "d", "e"). Of course, the result is the same.
Thanks RicardoMF. Your approach works beautifully. And very elegant too!
Presumably, gamemode is passed in the constructor and is set for each new object (car1=true or car2=false) when it is created.
I agree!!

Hello JohnV49

I completed the activity 3 thanks to your suggestions! Can you please explain the difference between the constructors for car1 and car2 in MyWorld class and the constructor of Car class? I am new to this...Thanks!
I worked with static variables for car1,car2,car3 and car4, in MyWorld class so I can differentiate different instances of cars from Car class.

The constructor method exists in the Car class to allow instantiation of a Car object.  MyWorld instantiates two Car objects, car1 and car2, by executing the (modified as mentioned) Car constructor method for each with the different parameter values that make them unique.
Thanks alot!!

just right click on the car class and "create new subclass".  I did this and it worked fine.  I do like the solution where you send the commands as an array to the object on creation for the controls better though.

After you have a new sub class create a constructor having the same name as the class and call the constructor of the parent class.  super(string1, string2)

then just copy cut paste the methods over that you want to have different and change the keys pressed. Keeping the same name of course.

Then in the world class you would create 1 Car and 1 Car2
How do I use two different constructors to initialize the same object? I've searched online and couldn't find the answer.

Shella5, I tried your solution, defining a public setKeyForward method in the car class. However, when I used car1.setKeyForward in the MyWorld class, the compiler came back that it couldn't find the method setKeyForward.

Hello there all of you nice guys,
thank you for all your comments, it helped me understand the code much better.

Also a special thanks to "RicardoMF" I created 2 implementations for the 3rd solution of the 2 cars problem.

Solution one: 2 classes, one for each car

Solution two: 1 car class, whereby I increased the parameters of each car object to include the car movements as well as the light switch, turbo charge and horn that I took over from the other exercise and took the solution of "RicardoMF".

So thank you all and greetings from the Netherlands.
Michael

@EdmontonJets and @RicardoMF, thank you.  This (keyForward attribute) works.  But why?

The method is called as part of a continuous loop through those methods?  (I don't know where that happens - what calls act()?)  Does each car have its own loop?
can somebody please post the whole code as i'm totally stuck?

retiredandnimble this makes sense to me. Duplicating classes and methods like the previous suggestions makes no sense at all and seems to go against everything we were told so far about OOP. I'm going to try you solution.
Worked like a charm :D No matter how many Cars we create, as long as there are keys left to be assigned! The keyboard is the limit lol. Thanks, retiredandnimble!

Could you please, paste the source code of your solution to try to understand it

I think you have answered this question. Can you explain the "archival mode"?

Public makes variables accesible to every class by casting them straight away like (from main) myClass.variable=2 but if you set them private you can ONLY change it by casting its methods like myClass.setVariable(2) and then in your method setVariable(int var) { variable=2;}. Declaring a method static means that you can write a method and it will be available within a class thos its got nothing to be with such class (youll need to write its code insidenthe class however), like MyClass() { static public int myMethod () { return 2; } static public void main (String[] args) { int a; a=myMethod.change();}} So your  variable a will be set to 2. Basically static means that can be global to be seen by other methods and or classes and private  or public means it can be accesed straight away by whoever just by casting a variable which means it has no access protection. I hope i didnt mess it up too much with my answer and i helpes you

In fact, you dont need any software for running a Java program.You can type the program in any text editor like Notepad and run that.Datails of how to do that is here http://www.skylit.com/javamethods/faqs/javaindos.html

well, i guess this refers to global and local variables isnt it? that's what i can pick from your explanation. may need to review the lesson again. However you ommitted the void keyword part, i may need more explanation on that.

oh! void means literally void, when you dont want to return any value back to a caller, you just use VOID. 2 examples:

1-public static void main (String[] Args): is set to void because it doesnt return any value (theres nobody to get the return anyway, Objext maybe but we dont need it anyway)
2-int a=myClassObject.myMethod(); where myMethod code is: public int myMethod(){ return 2; }, therefore a, will receive an int number (2). If you dont return anything, write void, otherwise, write the type of the value you want to return (a valirable type, value or a character). If theres a return, it needs to know what type of value is going to be returned

Unfortunately such actions diminish the value (credibility) of the certification for everyone taking the online course for credit.

You should mark your project as private. Just before the peer review begins on 6/16/2015 change it to public. Now it is open to the public just before peer review begins.

If you mark it as public anyone can look at it. It is not cheating.

So keep it private and change it to public just before the peer review begins.
Thanks for the info. I just found out that an Anonymous user has looked at my code.
@binarypulsar: I followed your suggestion, but I can't resist quoting 2 cliches: although this is "better late than never," it still is "locking the barn door after the horse had left."

There have always been cheaters and there always will be. I took a CS class where the professor would only credit students with a maximum score of 90% because he could could not confirm the homework assignment was actually performed by the student. Unfair for those of us who worked independently but on target for those who worked in groups and turned in identical code.

If it's any consolation, feel proud that you did it yourself and understand why many don't put much credence in a MOOC certificate. Not to belittle honest achievement but think about it...60% of the correct answers in this course earns you a certificate implying a mastery of the subject. Furthermore, there is no distinction between 100% vs 60%. It is one of the reasons why you can expect to have employers test your coding abilities when you interview for a job and more importantly, to feel confident that you will pass.

So as trite as it may sound, Julsey is correct "At the end of the day, people who cheat are only cheating themselves".

Good to see you posting here, Christian. A fairly simple solution would be to require a password for non-owners of a project to view it. That way you could include the password in the MOOC submission page so nobody could see it before the deadline.

Why can't this transition from private to public be automated?

Thanks for taking our concerns seriously, Christian. I hope I get to use Codeboard again in an online programming class.

@PhilWebster: yes, what we'll implement goes in the direction of what you're suggesting.

@Tunay: thanks, we're developing Codeboard as web-based IDE specifically for teaching as the requirements in an educational context are different from "professional developers". So we also hope that the tool proofs useful for other programming classes.

IMO the biggest problem right now is that it's possible to send a blank project and the first minute the time is over, you can copy from the people you are evaluating. As far as I know, you can't change the link you send to eDX, but during the week of assessment you can change your project as many times as you want, and nobody would notice it.

On the other hand, I love this way of evaluating. You have the code before your eyes perfectly indented, and you can compile and run it, even make little changes to the code without altering the original source.

So I would keep this system of assessing your peers.  However, it's always good to prevent cheating as much as possible, and don't make that easy!

Good point, Carlos. One way around that would be for the instructors to have the ability to set the project due date on Codeboard. Then the projects would be hidden until that time/date and locked after it so no changes could be made.

Another issue is if the projects are public after the submission date, then in subsequent offerings of the course with the same assignment, the submissions from the previous version might still be public.

Apart from whatever has been mentioned above, the IDE and the compiler in codeboard.io are pretty cool and better than majority of online java compilers. I'll be using codeboard more than any other online IDE'S. A very useful website!

I've assessed my 5 peers, but in my case I've found plenty of different bugs. The programs of my peers seemed to be 100% correct because their outputs were perfect, but if you looked their code, most of them didn't follow the rubric or didn't know how to comply with it. If somebody copy it's easy to get to 70% or 80%, but they'll never get to 100% because they don't know what they are doing. You only have to enter one bad input to the constructors or add one more employee to the staff and their program will be broken.

Yes, it's indeed a good online IDE, and the good news it's that not only you have Java to practice programming, you can also dabble in other languages, such as C, C++, Eiffel, Haskell, or Python. Furthermore, in Java and Python you can do Unit Tests.

@kwb

I hope your imagination is enriched now. The purpose of this course is that to learn something and i hope that you learned something now.

@kwb

Or maybe solution is too elegant?

"SOLVE THIS PROBLEM PROGRAMMING AN ITERATIVE METHOD"

I read this simply as meaning a method that uses iteration as opposed to recursion. 

It might also be a linguistic issue as this could also legitimately be read "solve using an iterative method" where method is used in the general sense of "means or approaches" (eg I use various methods when learning...").

All of the peers I reviewed had interpreted this correctly.

chances are that someone who used recursion didn't store values inside a loop and didn't really use a loop so points would be lost there. yet they should get excellent for calculation if it gave the right result and excellent for returning values.

@ugabrielu,

Thanks for the sample.

I'd have a lot more appreciation for your solution if it produced the correct results.

Regards,

@3gfeathers

Sorry, the bubble wasn't for all. By the way, I tried to keep code as simple as possible. The method call itself so technically it is recursive by "n-1", but I'm not interested what it produce, my goal is to get result from "for" loop. That's all. The swap is the most important thing for the loop, result from equation is an argument for itself while loop is running. Maybe it is possible to store result into array but you have to read this values with other "for" loop, this will complicating the problem. Why?

@3gfeathers

I'm afraid that you didn't understand the problem. So is not your fault because sample for lynx-snow share is wrong. Sample:
After 200 period the right result isn't 903.13, this is wrong, the proper result must be 953.88. Why? Because you calculate for a specific period, e.g. 200, initially you may say, OK my result is good, but be careful because you jumped directly to 200 period avoiding the other period, 1, 2, 3.... 198, 199, 200. So for a proper result you have to take in account even the other periods before 200. Why? Because of double precision variable type. In first year your number of snowhare isn't 270 but is 207.00000002. So if you jump directly to 200 period you will not calculate even this .000000002. So period by period this .000000002 will increase in value and after 200 period number of snowhare will be 953.88 not 903.13. You can't say that the snowhare occurred from nowhere directly in 200, 20 or other period, but you can say that population of snowhare increase and decrease period by period.
The only one solution to get a proper result is mine, I use same equation like you in a proper manner but we get different results. 
Your solution didn't use for calculation e.g. for 200 period witch is number of snowhare in 199 period, 198, 197, and so on. 
I will ask stuff for this problem, I think that I'm right on it.

By the way I think that peer assessment is a bad idea. My work is rated to 4 by my peers. Look at some of their comments:
"To complicated for me -with some comment"
"I found it so hard to read your code, it is bad coding practice.."
So this peers rate my work to 0 points. Is a madness.

I agree...you must take into account the previous periods and that is what the for loop does. It takes the previously calculated populations (of type double) at i (n) and uses them for the next calculation at i++ (n+1) and so on. That's what your loop does and that's what my loop does and that's what the staff's loop does so your point is not valid. One obvious difference is that you perform an additional calculation before even entering the loop of n iterations effectively performing n+1 iterations.

But more importantly...step back for a second and be objective. Have you considered the possibility that your code just doesn't execute the algorithm of the model as stated or that you misinterpreted the algorithm? It's more plausible than saying that your answer is the only correct one and that ~56,000 of your peers (and the staff as well) performed the calculations incorrectly?

no entiendo por qué d=26, no se supone que d= c+1? no debería valer c=27?igualmente en tu comentario dices que d=27

Estimados, de sus aclaraciones y mis operaciones de ensayo/error concluyo que la ubicación de los signos ++ y/o --, ya sea antecediendo o precediendo a la variable, varía el resultado me parece que esto obedece a una secuencia lógica como se mencionó en un comienzo.
En los siguientes ejemplos me explico mejor ...... favor sus aportes para aclarar el misterio... 

Caso 1

    int c = (2*3)+5*4;
    System.out.println(c);    //26
    int d = ++c;
    System.out.println(c);    //c = 27, ya que, ++C suma 1 a C    
    System.out.println(d);    //d = 27 es decir igual al nuevo valor de C

Caso 2

    int c = (2*3)+5*4;
    System.out.println(c);    //26
    int d = c++;
    System.out.println(c);    //c = 27, ya que, C++ le suma a C un 1   
    System.out.println(d);    //d = 26, es decir, no considera el nuevo valor de C

Me parece que el valor de c es el que se describe en el planteo, o sea 26, c no cambia su valor. Y si c = 26 entonces f = 25. Por lo menos eso creo.
Una pregunta se puede saltear la letra e en la asignación de valores?

Es simple, c++ es una forma corta de escribir c = c + 1, cuando asigno el valor a d queda d = c = c + 1, como hay dos asignaciones solo puede tomarse una y es la primera o sea d = c y descarta el = c + 1. En cambio si pones ++c seria igual a escribir c + 1 = c por lo que d toma el primer valor en la asignacion que es c + 1. 

Saludos

Hola, lo que ocurre es lo que menciona Coke321, los operadores ++ y -- pueden ser de pre/post incremento/decremento, dependiendo de si están ubicados a la izquierda o a la derecha de la variable. En el caso del ejemplo que son de post incremento, es decir, primero se asigna el valor de C a la variable D, y posteriormente se incrementa en 1 el valor de C. Para lograr que D == 27, debería escribirse D = ++C;

Hola, tienes razón al cambiar el ++ al principio y ejecutar correctamente, primero compilando y después ejecutando, si da el resultado que todos creemos. Era por el ++. sin embargo, el último resulto impreso, a mi me daría 57 y acá dice que es 59, tienes alguna idea de porque ocurre?

el valor de f, debería ser 25 puesto que 
int c= 26 y
 int f= --c (osea 25)

pues. creo yo, si hay una explicación diferente porfa compartela.

En Java, ++x es diferente a x++ 0 --x con x--.  Si x = 5  y c = ++x, entonces c = 6, debido a que cuando el operador precede a la x indica que primero realice la suma de uno y después la asigne a c.  Caso contrario el x++, c = x++, acá nos esta indicando que primero a signe el valor de la x a c y después le sume un uno a la x, entonces c = x++, c = 5 y x = 6.

Typical completion rates are around 5-10% (industry average is 7%). Given that this is a short course I'd think that there would probably have been somewhere around 5-10,000 submissions. As to how many have not received reviews, that is difficult to guess.

title: Extension of deadline for “Peer Assessment 1: reviews” until 16 June (11:59 am UTC)

children.body:
Dear learners,

Due to the large number of people that did not receive their grades of “Peer Assessment 1” in Week 3, we have decided to extend the deadline for submitting reviews until next Tuesday 16 June (11:59 am UTC). We encourage you to please review the tasks that have been assigned to you before the new deadline.

**IMPORTANT: please remember that those learners who submitted their activity, but did not grade their fellows, need to do it to complete the activity. Otherwise this will be considered undone and therefore they will lose a 10% of the final grade.**

Regards,

*Java MOOC* Team
You can't submit solutions anymore, that part of the assignment was due a long time ago.

@LoneFullmetal I just did a course on edx about c# and they started out with 2 assessments pp and later increased this to 3 because there where a few trolls in the course that just gave everyone 0 points. There were too many students for staff to review so their solution was the lowest of the 3 marks would just be dropped. I find 5 assessments also a lot tbh, but at least this way you have a better chance to get graded by a few students that know a bit more then nothing about java. But for me the best solution would jut be an autograderscript (JUnit) and a teacher explaining the solution after we finished sending it in.

Dear learner,

Remember the peer assessments have two steps. The one we reopened was the second one, the peer grading. The first one was due on 2 June.

Regards,

*Java MOOC* Team

Dear learners,

We have set up 5 gradings because of two reasons. In other courses of our university we created peer assessments with 3 gradings, and some learners complained about receiving low marks they considered unfair.

On the other hand, the final grade is not the average, but the mean of the grades, which means that the more grades, the more realistic the result.

Regards,

*Java MOOC* Team

Dear learner,

We are sorry, but if you did not do the first part of the activity, it is not possible to do it anymore. We have only reopened the second step, the peer grading.

However there is still time for exam 5 and peer assessment in week 5, so mabe you are in time to get the certificate!

Regards,

*Java MOOC* Team

> the final grade is not the average, but the mean of the grades

Actually, peer assessment on edX (in common with Coursera) uses the **median** value. This has the advantage of limiting the impact of outlying (or rogue) scores.

One of the reasons for having five assessors is to allow more flexibility for missing grades.
Staff doesn't care. You've failed because the other students were incapable of  completing the most basic tasks and the staff will do nothing whatsoever to help.
I think having a slightly bad grade due to trolls is better than having no grade at all and failing the course simply because the other students in the class are incompetent.

title: Clarification on c++ and --c
source_category: Week 1 / 1.4 Expressions and Operations in Java (non graded activity)

children.body:
I thought 	int d = c++; will be 27, since c is 26
		int f = --c; will be 25 since c is 26.
so why is this not the case?

I was confused by this too.  I played around with this by making the system print out the value of c, and saw that the statement d = c++ assigns d the value of c, but then changes the value of c to c+1. (Therefore, d = 26, and the value of c is changed to 27)  Then, the statement f = --c takes the new value of c (27), subtracts 1, and assigns that value to f, so f = 26 (and the value of c is also changed back to 26).

Hi, hope this help: 
c = 26

d = c ++ , result is c = 26 +1 = 27, and d return the before plus is 26. Simple remember is: d = c++ = c before plus = 26 

f = ++c, result is c = 27 +1 = 28 (27 take result from c++, not 26), f = c after plus  1 = 27 +1 = 28
Total result: c =28 (end result after all), d = 26, f = 28

___________________________ Try with c-- 

c = 26

d = c-- , result c= 26-1 =25, d = c before minus 1 = 26

f= --c, result c= 25-1=24, d= c after minus 1 = 25-1 = 24
Total result this: c = 24, d  = 26, f = 24

I noticed that c value is running until the end at f.
thanks for the clarification @latiku!
thanks v v much
@latiku  you're the man! thank you so much.
thanks for the explanation, it helped a lot
@latiku ty :)
Explanation very helpful, thank you.
@latiku thanks so much!
Great explanation. But if c becomes 27 than c%2 should be 1; but it's 0?
Same thinking as me. c=27 so c%2=1     hmmmmmmmm
I can't figure this one out either and it's driving me insane

Alright so at the line d = c++ it gets incremented by 1 so c = 27, then the next line it gets decremented by 1 so it's 26 again. 
4+3+26*2=59

A mi parecer la opción  "5 <=x ||x <= 10 " también es correcta, por que si lo comparamos con la opción " 5 <=x  && x <= 10 " es correcta, lo único que cambia es el signo de "or" y "and, pero a ambos lados de los signos se esta cumpliendo la sentencia y para "and" "true && true = true" y para "or" "true || true = true"

Nos piden que X este en el rango (5,10) **AMBOS INCLUSIVE**. Me parece que la opción correcta es 5 <= x && x <= 10 y solamente esta, porque las opciones  4 < x && !(x > 10) y la opción !(x < 5 || 10 < x) no contempla todo el caso planteado puesto que no dicen que también son iguales.  para 4 < x && !(x>10) no contempla la igualdad para el 10; para  
la afirmación  !(x < 5 || 10 < x)   tampoco contempla la igualdad para 10. Si lo colocamos en un programa al darle el valor de 10 a X nos daría fuera de rango.
yo puse la fórmula  !(x < 5 || 10 < x) en libreoffice calc y funciona correctamente

!(x < 5 || 10 < x) 
UNA PREGUNTA:
Disculpen mi ignorancia si les resulta incómoca.
Intento entender por qué !(x < 5 || 10 < x) es correcta si utilia OR ¿Acaso la negación ! al estar antes del paréntesis invierte el significado de OR y éste pasa a ser AND?
Tampoco entiendo por qué es correcta si no utiliza <= sino =. ¿Acaso todo lo incluido en el paréntesis es falso y por tanto su negación es verdadera?
Gracias anticipadas
José Antonio
Hola José, en efecto la negación de una disyunción (OR) es una conjunción)
Miro por todos lados "5 <= x || x <= 10", y no entiendo por qué es incorrecta. Nadie le ha encontrado una explicación?

La expresión "***5 <= x || x <= 10***" no es correcta ya que si, por ejemplo, reemplazáramos X=20 tendríamos el siguente resultado: 
**5 <= 20 (true) II 20 <= 10 (false)** 
recordemos que **true** OR **false** es igual a **TRUE**.
Pero el planteamiento nos indica que  **"x está comprendida entre los enteros 5 y 10"** por lo tanto nro. 20 sobrepasa el nro. 10 y no aplica al pedido.
el error, al menos el mio es confundir 5<=x con x<=5. no es lo mismo que 5 sea menor o igual a x, a que x sea menor o igual que 5. no se si me explico

Lo poco qwue entiendo Jaoche1154, es que al tener una negación de un valor, proporciona el contrario del operador, es decir que si teníais !(x menor que 5), lo convierte a X mayor a 5. Como tienes || en la operación, si alguno de los operadores booleanos es true entonces la condición es true.

Primero se evalúa todo lo que está dentro del paréntesis y  ese resultado toma el valor contrario dado que el signo ! (negación) está afuera del paréntesis, si estuviera entre el paréntesis sería otra cosa

Es falsa debido al || (or), recuerda que con esta condición con que uno de los dos sea true, la sentencia se cumple, así si tenemos que x = 1,  sabemos que 5 <= 1 da falso, pero 1 <= 10 es verdadero, como uno fue verdadero, ya todo es verdadero y al ser x  = 1 no se cumple la condición principal que x esté entre 5 y 10, incluyéndolos
Perdón pero si en el planteo dice que 5 y 10 están incluidos por qué en la explicación dice que el 5 no está incluido en el rango?
Se llaman leyes de Morgan, en lógica proposicional se ve eso, por si quieres checarlo.

jomarce, el tema es que usando el || que es OR, significa O, una disyunción. La sentencia de ( 5 <= x || x <= 10 ) tiene 2 condiciones, de las cuales se debe cumplir una sola (por el OR) y no ambas (eso seria en el caso del &&).

Entonces, lo que plantea es que
( 5 <= x || x <= 10 )
(CONDICION 1 Ó CONDICION 2)
O sea, se cumple que, ó 5<=x, ó x<=10, y necesariamente se deben cumplir las 2 para que x tome un rango que tenga extremos definidos, estableciendo que el punto de comienzo donde x debe valer 5 o más, pero no debe ser mayor a 10 por tanto puede valer 10 pero no mas.

También nos comentan que || se entiende como una intersección, por lo que cumpliria la condición. La intersección entre el conjunto formado por todas las x >= 5 y el de todas las x <=10, nos deja con un conjunto que expresaríamos precisamente por 5<=x<=10, aunque esta última expresión no es válida en formato Java. Esta duda me está torturando!

title: Opinions about the difficulty of the EXAM 3.

children.body:
I would like if those who already have programming experience, can give their sincerely opinion about the difficulty of this test.

To me it took quite some time to understand the exercises and I found it hard (this is my first course).

The opinion of a teacher and other newbies would be greatly appreciated as well.

regards
(sorry if there are errors in my english).

agreed. electrical engineering with basic and fortran as well. i've lost patience with the 'let's make you work hard and lose sight of what the material was you were supposed to be learning' approach.
already played that game, lost sleep, got my degree... now I want to learn a new language,  not play the old game again...

I don't really agree. It's hard for even experienced programmers to analyse code, let alone broken code, let alone broken code where the mistakes are contrived.  And the people doing this course are not experienced programmers.

The only questions that I missed were the Number of times question ( Question 4) and the second part of the last question.  In both cases even seeing the explanation of the answer I still feel lost on how the answers were obtained.   

My hat is off to you PauljM if you answered these questions in your head without resorting to writing them out or punching them in Greenfoot.  I took the time to punch these questions into Greenfoot and ran them with plenty of print and println statements so I could see how things worked.  If this had been a 2 hour timed test given in a classroom settings without access to Greenfoot I wouldn't have completed it.  

Perhaps this new world of object-oriented programming is for the young and not for those that were mainframe Cobol, Fortran, and RPGII programmers.

Recursion doesn't belong in an intro course IMV.  (1) it's conceptually difficult and confusing (2) It's rarely used in real life: including it in an intro course gives it too much emphasis and is likely to give beginners the wrong impression about programming.

LFos42: Why did you expect different?  In the unix world its often said RTFM. Or RTM as I like to say because I don't necessarily like the "F" character.

Did you pay anything for the course?

I think you have forgotten the standard of first year courses.  Besides, the problem is not with the course material, or Exams 1 and 2; just Exam 3, which is absurdly difficult and not well connected to the Week 3 course material. I say this as someone with degrees in engineering and math who earned his living for many years as a c/c++/assembly programmer on UNIX, PC and mobile devices.
BTW at this stage we've barely touched on the object oriented aspects of Java. That's still to come.

Peter_Donnelly: the standard of first year courses?  It depends on your major. Freshman seminar, In mine they told me to look around because only 1 of us would be left by graduation.  They were right.

Peter: What part did you find difficult.  I made my comments from my experience in CS courses at the college level.  They were harder then this course.  And they were 101 courses.

The exam is testing our understanding up to this point not necessarily just week 3 material.  I didn't see anything on the exam that wasn't covered in the prior material.

Been their, done that. As I have said I already have a degree in Engineering and a minor in Math and bad teaching is bad teaching. I am not interested in weed out courses, tricky exams, or people with programing experience taking introductory course and telling me how easy they are. The material isn't rocket science but it takes time to get comfortable with the jargon and structure of a computer language you are not familiar with and this class does a poor job covering these basics.

It is pretty obnoxious when someone with a degree in CS or has worked in the field talks about how easy this introductory class is. Let me give you a hint on to be successful in your later life. When someone gives you their personal prospective listen, you may learn something and really all its cost is having some humility.
I agree Peter. This is supposed to be an introductory course and teach basic concepts not a witch hunt to figure out what is wrong with a piece of code, or what the default return will be.

Why do I have different expectations? Because as I have grown and worked for a living I am no longer interested in a flawed university model that wants to weed out learners by making things more difficult then they have to be. Being unable to explain the material to someone is a sign of being incompetent, it truly takes a firm understanding of the material and how things work in the real world to recognize how to present it.

I liken it to learning the guitar where when I first started I had trouble finding someone who could place the material in content, it was only later that I realized that much of my early questions could have been resolved by a brief 10 minute overview on how the guitar is laid out and played. Life is hard enough without making it harder.

Well, I found strange that those reviewers that gave me excellent points have commented my code (with words of encouragement and congrats), but the ones that have taken points away hadn't. What gives? I have made comments to every single code under review. That's quite appalling.

I did my best to provide useful comments for any points I docked. I found the comments to be just a tad limited in character count to be as descriptive as I'd have liked...but I guess it kept me brief. Sometimes it didn't leave room for a lot of encouragement.

@juancarlos82 What do you mean by "half a program"? The instructions in this program only asked the students to submit a preyPredLV method. You have no reason whatsoever to take away points because one has not included a main method or a class construct.

Hi, thanks for comments. In fact I got the points right after my own review had finished. Despite of the fact, that some of the reviewers taken the points, I got the full mark. So, I think the final mark is calculated statistically.

I fully agree with you: I was over-generous too, taking into account that, possibly, for someone this is the first encounter into programming world. And in my comments I have shown how to correct the code.

> You have no reason whatsoever to take away points because one has not
> included a main method or a class construct.

Agreed entirely. Really one could argue that those who submitted a complete program were not following the assignment instructions. For those using the supplied template, it marks clearly the section to be submitted.

Absolutely agree! The code we were asked for was only a little part of the whole programme, if you didn't know that, you should read more carefully the next problem statements. Luckily, @juancarlos82 didn't remove any points for this reason, but maybe other people yes...

guys, dont get me wrong, i like people to make efforts and go for the extra mile. Just like H_sadah says, we had to submit a method, indeed, but methods come into programs which calls them. So, if i dont write the code thats missing in your program, theres no way to test if its alright, and mate, if i have to write code for you, id say your program is not working. I made mine complete and fully working. Why do i have to make others work to see if my code is ok? Some others made the main body, they werent lazy to spend literally, 1 minute to make a main body (it doesnt take longer, trust me). If your program works even without the main body, probably i gave you one point, but not all of them, wouldnt be fair for people who spend some time making the effort on programming the whole thing. Im sorry. As i said, i know we had to make a method, and probably yours was correct but its not implemented. Hope all of you guys are successful with this course because it worths it

I tested my own code which contained both the main and my preyPredLV method. When I received a peers work I used copy and paste to insert the method  right under my method. I changed my method name to preyPredLV0 so there was no conflict in method names. I was able to test/debug a peers code this way.   When I received a second peer's code I added it under the first peer's method and changed the first peer to preyPredLV1.  Eventually I have a single program with ten (I did more than the required 5 for a little debugging practice) additional modules that I could "switch on and off" easily or call independantly. Plus It's nice to compare multiple similar blocks of code.
I'm using a very stripped down "IDE" if you could even call it that called CodeRunner, but it does utilize the java components that allow me to compile and test code rather easily.   I just downloaded "eclipse" for java -- it may prove too complex for a noob like me to handle at this point...the the price was right so far at $0.00

@juancarlos82, obviously all of us had the whole programme in order to test it. Furthermore, the whole programme was accesible for everyone, because the staff published it in the problem statement, so you didn't need to write the whole programme, only copy/paste and check your own code (or your peers' codes). Of course you could write it on your own to do more practice...

title: EXAM 5 - UNDERSTANDING THE CODE - wrong answer

children.body:
The "Show answer" shows that the option "Any external class can access the method foo() from class UtilitiesSystem, prior to importing the package "myUtils.system". " is correct, but I think this is wrong. The declaration of the class is class UtilitiesSystem, so the class is private only visible inside the package.
Isn't this true?
foo is static, therefor you can access it like myUtils.system.UtilitiesSystem.foo(...). This syntax doesn't require import myUtils.system.UtilitiesSystem
Exactly, I posted the exact same thing right now, of course it will not work before importing the package. The vagueness of the words during this course has been troubling, to the least.
I agree. I'm a native english speaker. Some of the grammar is not correct and has led people to interpret the wrong answer. Ah, the beauty and frustration of the english language - bah.
I agree that the question is not worded properly.

Guys, this is an exam question, so it requires a bit thinking. Just like most exam questions, some time they're a bit tricky. That's the fun part, isn't it?

As "ttmai" had explained, one doesn't need to use the "import" statement to use the class or in this case a public static method. The "import" statement only makes it short to type out the whole class name.

I built a dummy Netbeans project, defined a package `myUtils.system` with class `UtilitiesSystem` in it.

Then defined `other` package, with `public static void main (String args[])` method in it.

When tried to access (and execute) `foo` method from `main`, Netbeans would complain that:

`"UtilitiesSystem is not public in myUtils.system; cannot be accessed from outside package"`

and throw an exception when trying to run `main()`.

On the contrary, if I define `public class UtilitiesSystem`, the code runs perfectly. Or, leave it _as is_ (__not__ `public`), but access it from any method contained in any class __inside__ `package myUtils.system`.

So, IMHO, this answer to the question is, at the very least, misleading. THe way `class UtilitiesSystem` is defined, It's __not true__ that _"Any external class can access the method foo()..."_, and therefore such option should __not__ be considered as a valid _"true"_ answer.

Hi,

I agree with abelinux.

The class has no modifier, therefore it is by default `package-private` and can only be accessed from within the package. No class from outside the package can use `foo()` even though this method is `public static`. I also tried to use the method from another class, and the compiler gives an error:

    Main.java:6: error: UtilitiesSystem is not public in myUtils.system; cannot be accessed from outside package
        myUtils.system.UtilitiesSystem.foo();
                      ^
    1 error
I'm in the same boat.  I interpreted "prior" to mean, "will this work before you import" or without importing it.  From what I've seen and tried, it will not work before the package is imported.

I'm with "juanfs". What else could "prior to" mean? 

The answer is wrong. I suspected the question was incorrectly worded because it seemed unlikely that they would include two options unless one was intended to be correct. But what do you do.
Aha !  Can we have a review please staff?

title: Rubic Child classes constructors Necessary Paramters

children.body:
Hi,
I am confused about the rubic for Child Class Constructors:
"The constructors receive only the necessary parameters
and properly calls super in the three classes."

1. What are the necessary constructor parameters for each Child Class?

2. In the Employee Constructor rubic it says the following:
"The constructor receives the four values and initializes the attributes calling to
the access methods"
Does that mean the Employee constructor must have four parameters?
When the child class calls the Employee Constructor what necessary parameters does it call it with: super(name, years. INDEFINITE, department)?

3. In the sample test code there was this Constructor call:
ManagementEmployee me = new ManagementEmployee("Bill", 9);
Are we supposed to only use these 2 parameters for the Management Employee Constructor? Why these two?

4. What about AdmistrationEmployee child class? What constructor parameters do we use?

Sorry about all the questions.
Just confused.

Thanks.

Thanks for your advice.

For the question: "What if you want to create Administration employee with training contract one day" there still be a constructor with 4 parameters there.

And also you can have several constructors in the Employee class as well. The compiler won't complain as long as it can pick up a correct one.

However, other just rang my bell is that I need to read the rubric and specification again to see if allows more than 1 constructor.

I appreciate the comments @marigold088 and @Rondzio but you never really answered my questions, you just told me your implementation, so still very confusing to me.

If I do not know what the necessary parameters are and the peer reviewer does not know what the necessary parameters are (or she/he has a different idea from me of what they should be) how can my implementation be fairly reviewed?

Shouldn't the instructor tell us what the necessary paramters are and we just implement it?
But how does the peer reviewer know this?

Specific employee classes are in fact "specialization" of the Employee. They share common things with other employees, but are given some conditions, like specific combination of attributes. So, at least, you should be somehow able to enforce one cannot create object that is "invalid" from this point of view. The easiest way to do it, is to limit possibilities right at the beginning by creating constructor of subclass that uses only selected set of attributes and the rest you fix by yourself. But I said "easiest", not the only correct one.
Anyhow, I am not so strict in peer reviews and I am always trying to find out how particular code was meant in the first place also taking into account, for some people this is obviously a first programming course.

Specialized constructors should be done in subclasses, not at Employee level. And yes, you limit the user, but that is how specialized employee is defined - it is some kind of contract, specialized employee definition has to fulfill. So its limitation on purpose.

ok. so for Management Employee I can:

1) set up constructor with name and years parameters ManagementEmployee(String name, int years)

2) call superclasss constructor with name, years paramters
super (name, years)

3) call setter to set the value for contract type this.setContract(INDEFINITE)

4) call setter to set the value for department type
this.setDepartment("Management")
ok. that is better. do not need to use setters. thanks.

Firstly, keep in mind that this is a teaching/learning exercise and not a real life practical application so the implementation may not always make sense. In other words, just follow the recipe.

Given that, my interpretation is that you are to use subclass constructors that only contain the parameters that are used in the call to the constructor. For example in the staff class, the call to instantiate the Administration employee has two parameters (name, department). So the Administration constructor should only accept those two parameters.

However, the subclass constructor calls super and  kwb is correct, the rubric asks that the Employee constructor receive four parameters. Technically you can do any number of things here to enforce the subclass restrictions but in this assignment you have to call super with four parameters so you might as well make them appropriate for each subclass.

Dear Shella5 and bribre,

as bribre said, the point of this first quarter is to make you aware of the problematic of using empirical tests to measure the execution time of which algorithm is best from a set of different implementations. It is used as  motivation for the concepts explained later in the week.

When you are trying to measure seriously the execution time of an algorithm (for example to run it on a real-time system where human lifes can be threatened if it does not meet a deadline), you should run it a lot of times and usually over an almost "naked" machine, that is, no windows system (in linux no X11), no unnecessary service daemons (such as printer and so on), only the kernel in linux and that's all. And even in this conditions, if possible, it is better to perform some static or hybrid WCET (worst case execution time) analysis technique (certification authorities, such as the European Aviation Safety Agency, rely on models to analyse the WCET in order to certificate a software with real-time requirements). It is a whole field of research here (and very important). 

If you want to know more about this:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Worst-case_execution_time

Moreover, there are commercial applications that allow you to perform a profile (memory used, execution time, etc), of your code. We have here the same problem, usually, they are profiling the paths that they are exploring with certain input parameters... Many times it is impossible to check all the possible inputs for a certain algorithm (only think on a simple algorithm that accept an integer, you cannot check all the possible inputs, as they are infinite).


But the point of the exercise was to give you an idea of the order of magnitude, not to evaluate seriously their performance.

bribre, the thread that you are talking about is:

https://courses.edx.org/courses/course-v1:UC3Mx+IT.1.2x+2016T2/discussion/forum/f815787b3379840fbfff5bd8fb497fdf4c289e5d/threads/57332da2d11155057e000ac8

![screenshot][1]


  [1]: https://edxuploads.s3.amazonaws.com/1463224649795149.png

Dear juancarlos82,

it would depend. If you are talking about an algorithm that would be used on top of a dedicated machine (such as one within a control system..) it only makes sense if you run the algorithm in the same conditions (the same hardware, software, etc), and only if you want an "accurate" measure. 

However, this is not practical, this is the reason why we use complexity measurements, profiling, modelling of the worst case execution time and so on.
I was talking about a special kind of systems: real time systems, the ones in which obtaining a correct result is not enough, but also the timing of this result (some examples, a soft real-time system would be all the media, where missing a deadline is not critical, some hard real-time systems avionics, control systems, automotive control systems, where missing a deadline implies that you jeopardise human lifes and where the measurement of the worst case execution time is critical).


Best regards,


Iria

I think I found the problem in my program. If a and b are both `double[2]` arrays, then `a = b` doesn't always give the correct behaviour. You have to do `a[0] = b[0]; a[1] = b[1];` or, more logically `a = b.clone();` (or even `a = Array.copyOf(b, b.len)` )

But this problem wasn't mentioned during the course (as far as I can find), and could well lead to confusion in the solution of this program.

Then I stepped through my code "by hand" and figured it out: The problem is changing values prematurely, i.e. you actually want to use the old value of an array variable in a later step but you have already updated the array variable to the new value.

In the computer, there are integers (which is what we have here), and real (floating) numeric data types. Integer types cannot hold the fractional portion of a number. So the decimal part of the number is discarded (the real term for this is "truncation" - we say the number is "truncated" - this is opposed to "rounded" where the value of the fraction is taken into account and the closest integer used). But the modulo function ("%") allows us to see what the remainder of the division operation is.

Hello guys! Not sure if the issue was resolved so adding a response. As 5 < 6, if you divide 5 by 6, you get 5 = (6 x 0) + 5. For % operator, you will always get the **remainder**   of the operation. Hence, 5 is the answer. Same for 1%2 as 1= (2 x 0) + 1 or 31%3 and 31= (3 x 10) + 1 we get answer 1.

31 / 3 = 10  = 10.333333

31/3= 10 (disregard the decimal since we're just finding the quotient)
31%3= 1 [since 31/3= 10 and 1 (which is the remainder)]
32/3=10 (disregard the decimal again since we're finding the quotient)
32%3= 2 [since 32/3= 10 and 2 (which is the remainder)]

So basically the operator "/" finds the whole number in or quotient in the problem and the operator "%" finds the remainder of the said whole number or quotient.

Yep, pretty much what we've learnt from the first week. You need to read and "execute" the code step by step, just like the computer would.

Like Beamsbox wrote, within the loop no variable is changed. Program checks if the condition of while loop is met. And it is: value of variable 'a' is less than 2093/2. So it executes the loop. Afterwards the condition is checked again. And since the loop didn't change value of 'a' nor 'num', the condition is met again. So is the one within a loop. And so the loop becomes infinite.

Dorukyalcin explained it quite well.

This will be a very important concept once we get into For and While loops... so if you don't understand it well... i highly suggest you take the time to research it and play around with the increments. :)

Hello klaypigeon,

You are right. In Java the ++ sign is also an asignment. It means add to this variable a 1 and store it again in the same variable : c = c+1. But when you have it **before** the variable, it makes the addition and the store of the value before the other statements. On the other hand, if you have the ++ sign **after** the variable, it will do the addition and the store of the value after the other statements. It is the same for --.

Regards,

Lucía.

Yes your code works by using a nested for_loop to print out all columns for all rows, that`s means print out any element [column ] from the 1D arrays stored in rows into a 2D array.

btw: this line is not used "**`int value = mx[row][col];`**"


if you want to read some about what is doing this line : 

    

>  for (int[]row : this.mx) {...}

is called "**Enhanced For-Loop**", it is the simplest syntax to traverse the whole array [ but only can be used, when you have necessary to read all elements stored into an array ]..

some documentation :

[**Oracle. The Java™ Tutorials. The for Statement**][1]

[**Using Enhanced For-Loops with Your Classes**][2]

[**The Enhanced For Loop**][3]


[**“Enhanced” for loops**][4]

[**Java - Loop Control**][5]

[**An enhanced for loop for the Java™ Programming Language**][6]


  [1]: https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/java/nutsandbolts/for.html
  [2]: https://blogs.oracle.com/CoreJavaTechTips/entry/using_enhanced_for_loops_with
  [3]: http://www.java-tips.org/java-se-tips-100019/24-java-lang/480-the-enhanced-for-loop.html
  [4]: https://www.cis.upenn.edu/~matuszek/General/JavaSyntax/enhanced-for-loops.html
  [5]: http://www.tutorialspoint.com/java/java_loop_control.htm
  [6]: https://jcp.org/aboutJava/communityprocess/jsr/tiger/enhanced-for.html
it looks a bit like a foreach loop in C#
no skills in c#, it should be the same...
Warning!!! Its not possible to use it for writing, just for reading purposes.

Enhanced FOR is not useful for writing, and its very easy to understand why. The reason is you equal each iteration of your array (right side of the for) to a same-type variable (placed on the right). It means you cant write in the same array or use its own possition (like array[i]) because you dont use a counter, i in a typical for. Unless you want to use a counter, then the enhanced FOR is not worth to be used. 
So, read, enhanced for, write, normal for. I do it like that,  but feel yourself free.

title: Algebra vs. programming ambiguity in the video

children.body:
Hi,
You used an expression of "x = x + y" in the video. In most cases it's unsolvable in algebra, but is correct in most programming languages when changing the variable's state. 
I think it would be good if you'd stated that explicitly, because such an ambiguity may lead to some misunderstandings.
BM.

It could be because "x = x + y" looks like an equation to somebody that is more mathematics oriented, so in the end, it just looks like y = 0.  Whereas for the programmer-oriented, it's one of the most natural and commonly used statements conceivable.

Not that I take issue with this, I just hope I was able to clarify a bit; I can't honestly speak for BM.

This type of expression exists long time ago even in old-school programming languages like BASIC. When you see an expression like x=x+y, your mind should interpret it as "the new value of x is now replaced by the old value of x plus the current value of y".

Perhaps a better expression in English:
..., your mind should interpret it as "the new value of x has become the current value of x plus the current value of y".

All variables have a value associated with them, this value is either explicitly set with an assignment statement (analogous to a Let statement in Algebra) or implicitly to either 0 or undefined (depending on context).

In the first time I got confused too, but the comments from **JImmyHo** make me clear about this. 

> there is a different approaches when using *pure algebra* and *programming*

I'm glad this was explained, I couldn't figure out how he just changed the meaning of x just because.  OK, so in other words with the programming expression this is just a continuous replacement.  If I understand.
the symbol "=" means assignment, its doesn't mean equal. In programing, "==" means equal!!!
Jimmyho,  Thanks for that clarification. I was baffled at how x could be the value of itself plus y.
So an array of primitives can be modified by a method call, but primitives, and strings, outside of an array cannot?

Thanks for the thorough explanation. So it doesn't matter an array is declared or not in the method argument, it can be changed by the operation within the method.

What happen if we don't want to change the original content of the array? Do we need to make a copy of it inside the method? Would the copy be discarded upon exit of the method?

Seems like you might also want to be able to pass strings by reference as well....if for example you have a fairly long string.  You could operate on a global string variable...but that places some interesting and perhaps inconvenient restraints on where the global strings are originally defined.  I guess it is...what it is.  Java works differently than "C" in that regard.

So the only way foo could change a local variable is through an assignment statement:
x = foo(x) and foo would have to be defined as 
int foo(int y){return y++;}
You could not write a method to change multiple local variables at the same time, given what we know now?
I thought that this was supposed to be an introduction to Java, yet instead of learning how to work with arrays, we are being introduced to this? Really!

Dear retiredandnimble,

no, we can't (in Week 3). However, we will learn in Week 4 how to use objects and this kind of variables opens a whole new world of possibilities :-)


Best,

Iria
@Cookie_Jarvis: My memory is that C is the same - pointer is passed, not the array. (This question is a little too advanced to be introduced at this point of the course IMO.)

title: Doubt about usage of super() function in the constructor of car, truck and motor bike class!

children.body:
In the above program, the program only runs when the constructor of sub classes have super() function.
I tried using color = c; lights=l; in the car, truck and motor bike classes but the code doesn't compile.

I thought that super() is only required in the constructor when the attributes of super class are private and we can't use constructors in sub class to create object directly.

Or does the abstract class automatically change the attributes to private??

please help!!

You are missing the basic concept of inheritance. Per my example above. class B extends class A. When you create an instance of class B all it's superclasses are also created. You can visualize this like layers, one class on top of the other with superclasses beneath subclasses.

**The default constructor is supplied by the compiler only if no other constructors are defined.** This is why some classes do not explicitly define a default constructor. **Once you define a constructor with arguments, the compiler does not automatically generate default constructor, you will need to explicitly define one.** You can test this by defining 2 classes, 1 with no constructors and 1 with a constructor that has parameters. Then try to instantiate both those classes using default constructor syntax.

As per your question:

> public Motorbike(String c, boolean l){ color = c; lights = l; }
> 
> instead of
> 
> public Motorbike(String c, boolean l){ super(c,l); }

The answer is in my previous post.
The 1st code fragment is only valid if Vehicle has a default constructor. Simply, the parent instance Vehicle of Motorbike has not been created yet, so there is no color or lights attribute.

The 2nd code fragment is only available if there is a constructor in the superclass that matches parameter signature (String, boolean).

Many thanks for explaining the concepts very clearly. 

I couldn't find these concepts in the video or maybe I overlooked it. Can you recommend some resources for some more explanation of this concepts.

Thanks for taking time to clear my doubts.

setColor should follow the same logic as setLights: setters make no sense to return any value but to set it. So define it as public void setColor(String c){
    color = c;}
Because return type of setLights is void so u don't need return statement.But setColor has return type String,so u have to return String

@ttmal: you're explanations are great. Just to make sure I understand correctly. I define parent class A with a constructor which has arguments (so it's not a default constructor). Now I define a subclass B that extends class A. The very first line of constructor of class B hast to be 'super' with appropriate arguments (dummy ones or not), right?
As I understand it is because class A has to be initialized and in order to do so, I need to call its constructor.

@piotr007: In your example, class B  constructor can have empty or 1 or more parameters. The super call in class B constructor can have empty parameter list if class A has a default constructor. In your example, class A doesn't have a default constructor, so that code will not compile. If class A didn't define any constructors, the compiler will supply a default one that does nothing.

However, if class A has a defined constructor with same number of arguments and types in same order, the super call in class B will not produce compile time error.

@ttmal: In your first example,you mentioned It has to instantiate Class A before instantiating Class B.But the Vehicle is a abstract class and in the video lecture it is mentioned that abstract class cannot be instantiated.Please Clarify.

Why do we need to declare public abstract double getSalary() inside Employee class? Since its declared inside Employed interface it is already public and abstract and every subclass extended Employee class must implement it. I guess "public abstract double getSalary()" line inside Employee class is unnecessary. Convince me otherwise.
I do compile it. Just comment the line //public abstract double getSalary() ant try

You are correct in case you make Employee class *abstract*, but is matter of good practice to show it explicitly - looking at the definition of class, you see all your subclasses need to implement. But you are not forced to do so.
Thanks, it makes sense.

Well, I ask myself the same question. As far as I understood we are not supposed to declare getSalary in the Employed class. Instead we are advised to create the interface Employed containing the method getSalary, and then implement it in the Employee class. I wonder why this is necessary. If you asked me I would just put an abstract declaration in Employee and then implement it in the subclasses of Employees. To me, hat seems much easier and elegant.

If I am not mistaken, the suggested procedure has to do with design. But when I create an interface and implement getSalary in Employee I have a declaration that I don't need because the real salary is calculated in the subclasses. So I put the method in an interface, implement it in Employee and then write the implementations that matter into the subclasses. This is one step too much, isn't it?

Does anybody understand why I need to complicate things with all this extra code.

For slightly more details on why are interfaces interesting and useful, see comments in this post: [here][1]
In this exam, use of Employed interface is little bit much, but you need to show you understand how to work with them as part of course. [1]: https://courses.edx.org/courses/UC3Mx/IT.1.1x/1T2015/discussion/forum/d8f8b6fb8967c2ad6aef91985a7d8b582aadf331/threads/55703086384206fffc000098
I guess that source staff just wants us to use the Interface as java advantages even though it's not too elegant in this specific task.

There is definitely a slight disconnect between the framework of the problem, and the grading rubric. The problem says implement Employed interface, but also says there should only be 5 classes(with Employed there is 6). The rubric says Employee should be declared abstract and getSalary should be abstract (so no need for Employed).
and says ddont create more methods either so you cant even create them at subclasses. I made a "central" method at Employee and theres where alll the calculations takes places

There's no mystery to make work a file with all the Java code in it, the only restriction is that you can only have **one public class** inside the file, which is usually where the main method goes.

In principle, I get that it's recursively getting back up to 4 (for printLine), but where/how is this incremented value stored? what is the "prior execution"? doesn't it always go down to zero and return 1 to printLine?

sorry for being such a dunderhead. i'm using a jackhammer on my brain and looking on the Web for recursions, but it's slowing me down.

anyway, thanks again.

http://www.codeskulptor.org/viz/index.html

This is Python but the mechanism is the same. Somehow I can't save in Viz mode. So go there and paste this code in. If you don't see the 'yellow' step buttons, hit the 'tool' key first. Delete the template code and then paste in. Keep the indentation, Python delimits blocks by indentation.

    def printLine(width):
        for i in range(width):
            print "*",
        print
        
        
    def printTriangleB(height):
        if height <= 0:
            return
        printTriangleB(height - 1)
        printLine(height)
        
    printTriangleB(4)

Set a breakpoint at the last line by clicking the line number. Hit run (the play button) and then single step through by clicking the right arrow. The frames and their content are displayed in the right area.

![enter image description here][1]


  [1]: https://edxuploads.s3.amazonaws.com/14512199418488015.png

> but where/how is this incremented value stored?

That was my main problem at first too. The values seem to fall from the skies...

For each function/method call a new environment (stack frame) is created and the value(s) are passed to it. This environment is kept in memory until it's return statement or end is reached. You can see that in CodeSkulptor Vizmode.

title: why 21 and not 20?

children.body:
For the question: 
String s = "Learning Java is fun!";
What is the value printed on screen by the following statement?

Why is the answer 21 and not 20?

Hello Danny,
thank you for your reply :-)
why not 22 coz counting double inverted comma(") its comming 22

The string length is anything between the quotes "".
The ; is the end of the string assignment statement in java. It does NOT belong to the string variable s.

i though we start counting from 01234 etc and not 1234 etc
if you count from 0123 etc, you will get 21
but if you count from 1234 etc you will get 20.
so which one now is the right way to start counting and from the lessons so far we start counting from 0

you must start with 0 when you count;
L=0 e=1 a=2 r=3 n=4 i=5 n=6 g=7 space =8 J=9

@Kcspex

Counting and length are two entirely different things.

So... in your name:

    0 1 2 3 4 5 
    K c s p e x

BUT...

Your name is 6 chars total in length... the zero or first position is still meaningful!

If you are doing 5-0, to determine the length in this example, that's taking the *difference*, NOT taking the *total* size!  Make sense?

The last char is in position 5 of the string.

The string is 6 chars overall in size.

I hope this helps!  :)

I don't understand why 5 <= x || x <= 10 is wrong. If x == 10, then it is correct. Can someone explain me this?
Also, if x is 0, the second part of the expression is true, which means that the final result is true. This is incorrect since 0 is not between 5 and 10.
Thanks for the explanation guys! Helps me a lot!

Remember, you cannot arbitrarily choose only one value for x to consider.  It must work for ALL values of x.  Look at it this way: Will it work for these three cases: when x is any value less than 5; when x is any value between and including 5 and 10; and when x is any value larger than 10?  If it works for ALL three cases, its correct.  Otherwise, it is not.  Quickly, we see it fails when you plug in any number not between 5 and 10.

I hope this helps.
For example, x = 4; 5<=4 -> false; 4<=10 -> true; then, false || true -> true.

x cannot be any value less than 5, x is suppose to be any integer between 5 and 10, 5 and 10 inclusive. which mean x is either 5 or greater than 5 but less than 10 or equal to 10. I'm sorry I think your analogy up here might be incorrect.

title: Doesn't make sense
source_category: Week 1 / 1.2 Calculators with various data types

children.body:
(n<m) && (n<=m) equals (n<m)

First we are dealing with T/F and then jump into expressions.  I don't understand this statement
"(n<m) && (n<=m) equals (n<m)"
(n<m) && (n<=m) equals (n<m)
Since "(n < m) && (n <= m)" will only be true if "(n < m)" is true, and since "(n <= m)" will always be true if "(n < m)" is true: it can be simplified to "(n < m)".

RMVGaines, 
I was going to respond, but you beat me to it. 
Then I was going to upvote your reply, but it seems like replies don't have a botton for upvotes! Very strange!
Thanks!

In that example, for both (n<m) AND (n<=m) to be true, you can only accept all values of n which are less than m. If you include the value of n that is equal to m, the expression (n<m) does not cover that value. Therefore, you are not taking BOTH (n<m) AND (n<=m) into account. 

Think of it as a square and rectangle. If I need to understand an expression that would describe both shapes, a rectangle would be sufficient as a square is an equilateral rectangle. Conversely, a rectangle is not a square by any means. Therefore, we can only use the word rectangle to describe BOTH a square AND a rectangle.

The Boolean part of this has to deal with the evaluation of the expressions. A computer will evaluate the n<m and then the n<=m and given the "&&" will return n<m.
I agree if replies had an up vote they would be easier to see as shown with sites like stack exchange.

title: What Do They Represent?
source_category: Week 1 / 1.4 Play with the ternary operator (non graded activity)

children.body:
What does the argument b?false:true mean? 

That there is an argument "b", and it can be true, false, or undefined? I do not really understand this, if anyone can shed some light, I would be grateful. 

I understand a classic truth-table, such as:

a   b   a&&b  a||b
t   t    t     t
t   f    f     t
f   t    f     t
f   f    f     f

I also understand that in the case of the && operator, if one variable is false, the other is not necessary to evaluate, since it will be false. Likewise, with the || operator, if one variable is true, the other variable does not have to be evaluated since it will also be true. 

But the language b?true:false seems cryptic to me, I have re-watched the previous video twice already...

Thank you for the response. 

So in the case, **b**?*true*:*false*.

B is true, and the false statement afterwards represents what? Its own negation?

Also, in the statement b?a:true--what does this mean? b is true, or a is true?

b?true:false is an expression that outputs a Boolean value. b is an argument. If b is true, the expression outputs the second argument, which is true. If b is false, the expression outputs the third argument, which is false.

Similarly, in b?a:true, a and b are arguments. If b is true, the expression outputs a, and if b is false, the expression outputs true.

Also, these expressions do not tell anything about a and b. The expressions just take a and b as arguments in order to output a Boolean value.

Okay, I understand that they are expressions, and I understand that they output a boolean value, but what do they represent--let me try to ask in a different manner...

In the chart that we complete for activity, "Play with the ternary operator (non graded activity)", which is the first activity under Week 1.4. 

Why is the first block under column b?a:true said to be true, and the block underneath it said to be false?

In the first block, b is false. This means the operator returns the third argument, which is true.

In the second block, b is true. This means the operator returns the second argument, which is a. a is false in the second block, so the operator returns false.

Maybe look at it like true=left and false=right

b=left:right

So if b=true, the only thing we care about now is what is left.  And if b=false, the only thing we care about now is what is right.

In the example you brought up b was false.  So look to the right.  Whats at the right?  True.  So bring True back to B.

In the one below like you brought up well b is now false.  So we look to the left.  Well that's A.  What is A.  They changed A to false...well just bring that back to B.

I hope that helps a bit.

Wow! Thank you a lot, Student815! 

I see it now, so then to evaluate something like b?a:true.

1. Check b, is it true or false? 
2. If b is true, check argument left of the colon, which is a, so return the value of a.
3. Else if it's false, check argument right of the colon, which is true, so return true. 

Thank you very much! 

One more question though, what if it's undefined?
thanks it really helped.

A good way to recognize spaces is to highlight the text character by character. That way you could count quotes including a space like so: **"** *(1)* **space** *(2)* **"** *(3)*. Hope it helps!

Actually, since charAt returns a char instead of a String, if the + "" + part was missing, the result would be very different. By concatenating the empty String, the two chars get promoted to Strings.

That makes sense. There is only one problem. In the example for including backslash (2:15 in the video) in the string, they wrote **"\ \ and \ ""** . What is the output of that string value? **\ and "** ?

And from another angle. The quote is interpreted by the computer in such a way that the computer will think anything INSIDE two quotes is the string. Now if you have lots of quotes inside each other it will confuse the computer and maybe it will try make lots of different string variables. Basically your code would not even be allowed to run.

The solution to make the computer ignore those inner quotes is to put the slash in futon of them. Of course when you finally see the string appear on the screen the slash won't be visible. It's simply there so the computer does not think it's a new string you're creating.

title: No esta bien formulada la pregunta.
source_category: Semana 1 / 1.1 Calculadora sencilla con pantalla para expresiones

children.body: Ahora, ¿puedes convertir 23ºF a grados centígrados usando la calculadora anterior?¨ Esta es la pregunta propuesta por ustedes y la respuesta seria con un si o no. No especifica que resuelvan la operacion.
Puede que la caja solo acepte valores numéricos. Si escribiste "-5 °C" quizá tome el valor como texto.

Ya está resuelto, solo que la calculadora no tiene la capacidad de interpretar el signo negativo dentro de la operación, como, por ejemplo:
-9*5
como 23-32 es igual a -9
luego intenté efectuar -9*5 y allí descubrí la excepción.
La caja acepta el valor númerico y es correcto -5, aunque predeterminamos que son grados Farenheit de acuerdo a la pregunta.
porque un numero entero ?
POR FAVOR, CUAL ES LA RESPUESTA CORRECTA?

Tal cual es lo que pasa:

 1. No se limita ni explica la forma como se debe interactuar con la calculadora.
 2. La calculadora es muy permisiva respecto a lo que se puede escribir.
 3. La calculadora es muy limitada respecto a la forma como pueden realizar las operaciones.
 4. Las excepciones igualmente no están bien validadas.

Por otra parte y dándole crédito a lo que se persigue con el ejercicio, es posible que darse cuenta de los errores o limitaciones en una aplicación sea lo que se quiera resaltar y que finalmente son consideraciones que debemos tomar en cuenta para nuestro propios desarrollos.
Una de las ideas básicas del ejercicio es recordarnos que hay que leer detenidamente el enunciado. Ahora son 23 grados no 30 como en el anterior y además debemos convertir de Fahrenheit a Celcius.

title: The last number I get is 57. Why am I wrong (if))
source_category: Week 1 / 1.4 Expressions and Operations in Java (non graded activity)

children.body:
My logic for last line is:
-    c=26, so f=26-1=25
-    25*2=50
-    50+3+4=57. 
So where 59 comes from?
And if to check (f*2) it gives 52, and (f*1)=26. Why f=c when it is given --c and not c--?. And why is c--=27?
thanks! although there is no condition where c=c-1 so it is not clear to me where you get this rule
f=--c is the same as c=c-1   f=c-1
but this is f=c-1, not c=c-1

c, d, and f are all 26
In this line "c=(2*3)+5*4" c is assigned the value 26

In this line "int d = c++;" d is assigned the value 26 **THEN** c is increased by 1 to become 27

Finally in the next line "int f = --c;" the value of c is **FIRST** decreased by 1 to 26 before f is made equal to it, so f becomes 26 also

So finaly "System.out.println(3+4+f*2);" would be 26x2=52,  52+3=55 and 55+4=59

Hope that helps   ;-)

int a = 0;
		int b = a + 1;  //b =1
		int c = (2*3)+5*4;  //  c =26
		int d = c++;  //d=c   d=26 en c = c+1 = 27
		int f = --c; //f = c-1= 27-1 = 26
		
		System.out.println(d);  //26
		System.out.println(d+1); //27
		System.out.println(c%2);  //0
		System.out.println(3+4+f*2); //59

because c++  is a postfix. that means that the value of c++ is c before adding 1, thus d(=c)=26. Then c increases by one and the new c=27. It's actually explained quite well in the video. Check the manuscript of the video and watch it again. Hope this helps :)

title: The key to understand it...
source_category: Week 1 / 1.4 Play with the ternary operator (non graded activity)

children.body:
You must remember the explanation about the ternary operator:

**b ? e1 : e2**

*It means:* 

 - if **b** is **true**, then evaluate **e1** (and forget about e2).
 - if **b** is **false**, then evaluate **e2** (and don't even think about e1).

*In short:*
if b is true, evaluate the first one. If it's not, evaluate the second one.

Hope it helps.

I think you are asking what is '?'.  (What is the question mark?)

That is the ternary operator.  I think the easiest way to explain it is it means **'then evaluate'**.  Going off of HugoEsteves above:

b ? e1 : e2

 1. if b is true, **then evaluate** e1 [if b is true **?** e1]
 2. if b is false, **then evaluate** e2 [if b is false **?** e2]

Its just telling the computer, "Hey computer, if B is **true** look at the thing on the **left**"

Or telling the computer, "Hey computer, if B is **false** look at the thing on the **right**"
