Does the recursion really changes the quality of the code in cases like this one? I mean, do I get a speed advantage when computing or a shorter code? I don't see the advantage of it. Thanks guys!
I think the "/" and "%" word in Java are defined with a recursive function.  So if you are using this  words, you are actually  using a recursive function.

I have also come to the same solution as SaraAlves and I think it's perfect and elegant solution. I know that this chapter is all about recursive methods, but nevertheless I think it's quite nonsensical to try to use recursive method here. It would be as if someone asks you to use recursive adding function instead of simple multiplication!

> I think it's quite nonsensical to try to use recursive method here

The learning objective in this section is recursion, hence it is perfectly sensible to use recursive methods. In the same way, other courses use recursion with successive approximation (the Newton-Raphson method) to calculate square roots even though there exist perfectly fine square root functions in the language.

Division is taught (or was back when I was a boy) using repeated subtraction--albeit using an iterative rather than recursive method!--so the basic principle is sound.

title: Why setters and getters in the same class?
source_category: Week 4 / 4.4 Improving the Project in Greenfoot

children.body:
I understand you need setter and getters to access private variables outside the class. But, why in all these examples are setters and getters being used when it can be accessed directly. Even if it is private, a class can access its own variables.

Can anyone explain why to me? Thanks.

Thanks, Andy! That makes sense if you want to add something extra like a counter to keep track of how much a setter or getting is being called. Thank you for pointing that out.

However, if there is no extra coding going on like counts and it is just simple assignment of private variables, why is it necessary then? To me, it seems much easier to figure out if it's all direct (assuming no extra code like counters). Otherwise, I have to go and check each setter method if I was looking at someone else's code.

Also, another concern: keeping your private attributes private. If we define getters and setters for every class this way, then aren't we losing the whole point of setting the variables private? I mean, another class then simply use a setter method to change variables they should not. Thank you again for your insights!

However, when you declare a class u might want to pass values. Thats alright but if you want to rewrite any value n get any value from such class, and the variable are set private, youll need to call a set and get method in order to do that cos otherwise u wont be able to see the variables from the class (behaviour protection)

Thanks Reynaj!

Your example where we need extra code to verify the parameter or Andy's example from above where we need extra code to set up counters makes a lot of sense. In those cases, I can definitely see the value of creating a setter method like you both described.

But what about those cases where we don't need or want any extra code? Where just want to do a direct assignment like number = num (where here num can be any value and all inputs are valid)? A second concern, if we set up getters and setters for every private variable even when we don't need/want any extra code (like counter or parameter verifying), then aren't we losing a benefit of having private variables? We made them private originally so that other classes can't set them, but via the setter method, they can.

The thing is that nobody can be able to access variables in an uncontrolled way, thats why you set them private and onky methods from such class can change it but anyway, you dont really need to change every variable like an i inside a loop so you dont need to declare any set method for that. Use only code whenever you need it so your program is lighter, robust and not embarrased or hard to follow. Whatever parameter  you might need to change after declaring a class, try to do it using a set and fetch results or values using gets
Note that private variables are not private inside its class but global, not so when called from the outside (like main)
If you want the variables to remain private and not be set or get from outside the class.... you could just make the get and set methods private yes?

Prepend the package, e.g. acme.Staff.java and acme.Staff respectively. In other words, the path is relative to codeboard.json file.

Hola JaimeSierraB,

Siempre estuvo en negrita :)

Aunque ahora no pasa nada porque es un ejercicio que no cuenta para tu progreso en el curso, sería importante que entendieras correctamente los enunciados de los exámenes. Pues esos sí afectan a la nota.

Lucía.

Hola Willyam,

5%6 es 5 porque si haces la división entera, 5 no cabe en 6 y el resto es 5. Lo explicaré con un ejemplo:

Si tienes 10 caramelos y vas a repartirlos a tus 3 amigos en partes iguales, puedes hacer 3 grupos de 3 caramelos cada uno. Así tus amigos se llevan 3 caramelos, pero hay uno que no encaja en ningún grupo de caramelos, te sobra. Ese caramelo es tu resto, así 10/3 (en división de enteros) es 3 con resto 1, por lo tanto 10%3 es 1.

Espero que eso lo clarifique.

Saludos,

Lucía.
OK, gracias, Lucía.

I will try to give you an answer of what I suppose to be your question;) The Apache license (see http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0 , last paragraph of section 4) explicitly states that the creator of a derivative work (modified version) may license that derivative work under a license other than that applicable to the original work. The application of different license terms (which may include proprietary license terms) is permitted so long as the terms of that license comply with the Apache license of the original work. The question in exam 5 gives us different licenses under which we may or may not publish our program.
To answer this question, we should ask for every option: "Does this license comply with the Apache license?"
This means: **Will the new license respect the obligations of the Apache license** (read carefully section 4, a to c !) If this doesn't answer your question (means: If I answered the wrong question), please feel free to post again and I'll give you my email address - (Exam 5 is still open....)

Hi bribe,

These licenses are really heard to read and understand!
These are legal texts, written for lawyers and not for "normal" people. To get a feeling what is meant, I suggest to go to the [creatif commons webpage][1] and play around with different types of CC licenses. They show also a human-readable version that we can compare with the actual license texte itself.
After having compared different types, it's easier to understand other licenses that doesn't offer such a human-readable version.

I'm having a hard time understanding how this works. So a is a random variable. We assign the value a to x, x is no longer 1 but a, we assign value y to x which we previously turned into a so now y is also a? Or does this mean that y is the original x, 1?

1) Start with: x=1 and y= 2

2) Set x=a 

if x=a then a=1

3) set x= y

if x=y then x= 2 (you just swapped the value of x)

4) set a=y

if a=y then y=1 (you just swapped the value of y)

I think the misconception that is probably leading to your confusion is. ...  x does not change. x retains it's value of '1' because the value of x has not been altered, a has been altered. SO. .. after a=x is executed, both a and x are = to 1. Hope this helps.

My opinion is that, when ever the variable x is declared (e.g int x) in a method, it is a local variable (x in change1() and first cury bracket in test() ie line 3 and line 13). But if x is only assigned without a declaration (line 7, 17 and 19) it is refering to the global variable x, thus any modification made changes the global variable.
sir, aside from the topic, at the sixth minute 13th second, an equation is shown in the background as x=x+3, i believe that is an incorrect statement and should not be used as an example.

I believe this is not an example of an equation, but of an assignment statement.

Suppose we have a state in which {x=2}.

When the computer reads the statement x = x + 3, it will calculate the expression x + 3 based on the state {x=2}, so 2 + 3 = 5.

It will then store 5 in the variable x. From this moment on, the state will be {x=5}

Setting "-Xlint:unchecked" means : Turn off the warnings.    
As a programmer, we really shouldn't turn off the warnings.
Note: Recompile with -Xlint:unchecked **for details**.

Sorry, I was wrong - and thanks for your patience! "-Xlint:unchecked" doesn't mean turn off the warnings. It means: Recompile the code with this option. It's done from the command line (terminal): javac -Xlint:unchecked
But if you're working with eclipse, you find the details of the warning - and errors - under the problems tab. ![][1] To configure what error/warnings are shown, we can change what kind of warnings are shown in the popup we get when choosing «Configure Content» from the dropdown on the right: [1]: https://edxuploads.s3.amazonaws.com/14954534225625057.png

In IntelliJ, the warnings are displayed when you hover over the yellow highlighted parts of the code: ![][1] We can also enter command-line options in the popup we get from → File → Settings →Build, Execution,Deployment → Java Compiler: ![][2] The output is then shown in the messages panel: ![][3]

I am retired.  I graduated as an Electro Mechanical Engineer in Uruguay and immediately accepted a job and married. I was trained for almost one year in Buenos Aires,  Argentina and then transferred to New York City where we spent 41 years,  became American Citizens and had a son.  We both worked and retired.  Then,  in 2006 we moved back to my hometown,  where we had met and married.  My wife is originally from Asunción,  Paraguay.

We enjoyed for some time helping as volunteers with OLPC,  One Laptop Per Child.  Now there are many professional teachers trained with the program and they hardly ever use the help of volunteers. 

Computers were not part of the curriculum in engineering school in my time but,  we used them extensively and intensely during our working years.  The part of our working lives we enjoyed the most was selling Real Estate in NYC where we were leaders in our geographical area in the use of computers.  We created our own website in 1997 and used it with excellent results to advertise the properties we had for sale and to build up and maintain our prospects database.

By constantly playing with and improving our database (dbase III),  we were able to concentrate our advertising budget in the best prospects.  We farmed a 15,000 home area of NYC,  around our own home.

We are enjoying retirement traveling to visit our son and his beautiful family in the Charlotte, NC area.  We have taken three beautiful ship cruises since we retired,  taking us as far as St. Petersburg, Russia.  We have reserved our fourth cruise for later this year,  out of NYC,  through the Caribbean and ending in Fort Lauderdale, Florida, to continue by train to visit our son and his family.

I am enjoying very much the online courses from famous universities I dreamed of attending many years ago.  I completed Computer Science 101 in Stanford,  This week I plan to finish College Writing in Berkeley.

Nice to meet you Qadir.  It sounds like you are taking good advantage of the online course by getting together with others. I have never visited Pakistan but have met some wonderful people from there.  We have been in touch with the Mirzas for over 40 years.  Mr. Mirza worked at the United Nations in New York City while we lived there.  His wife was a teacher in the U.N. School where our son was a classmate of Khurram, their son.  After graduating in New York University,  same as our son, Khurram worked in many places,  as far from us as Dubai and about a year ago he and his family moved to North Carolina and live about one and a half hours from our son´s.  We see them quite frequently.

qadirb,  you seem to have the formula for success: If one avenue doesn´t lead to where you want to go,  try a different one but,  never give up.

By the way,  something funny.  If you didn´t understand one of my comments, this is why: Somehow instead of reading your original message,  I read this abbreviated version,  that appears above: "Hi, nice to see you here. I am Qadir from Pakistan, 40 male trying to learn Java through self-study and online courses like this this. I hope to learn a lot here.".  Instead of understanding you are a 40-year-old man,  I understood you joined a group of 40 men who decided to take this course together. Ja! Ja!

qadirb, yes, I have started going through the courseware. This week is a bit special for me. I am still doing the last week of the College Writing course at Berkeley.  I am noticing now the time differences with my classmates. Uruguay where I live is 4 hours ahead of Berkeley, California and about 5 hours behind Madrid. I used to be among the first learners in Berkeley to read the assignments and now by the time I get up in the morning,  I am well behind many of my classmates.  It is 8 AM here now;  what time is it there?

glasachin, nice to hear from you. Interesting the mix of languages we read in a course like this.  I believe it is one of the many pluses of what we are being offered. English is not my mother tongue either. I imagine both qadirb and you learned british English. Mine is american English. Many years ago,  I was in London hearing one of my business associates describing me physically to one of his colleagues in Oxford who was expected to meet me on my arrival at the train station.  This was the description:"He looks mexican and speaks very good English with an American accent". I was always told my accent is Spanish which was no surprise since that is my mother tongue.  I never thought that behind my strong Spanish accent, someone would clearly detect that it is american rather than british. My looking mexican didn´t surprise me;  I thought it was because of the prevalence of mexican movies at that time among Spanish language films.

Seniors in America means much older than qadirb´s 40 years. The decision of using the term "Senior" is usually based on the benefits that come up with age, like paying less at the movies or for the bus or,  the most awaited benefit, Medicare, the government sponsored health insurance, I believe Medicare starts at 62 but not quite sure because I have already enjoyed it for many years.

Hope you both and all classmates accept my familiar language, on a first-name basis,  which is quite standard in the U.S.A. in situations like ours.

qadirb, glasachin,  yes, of course, it is okay to address me as Carlos. For many years we were in business as Real Estate brokers.  We advertised as "Carlos & Nenny Rabassa" and asked people to remember us by our first names. First names were quite widely used,  among many other reasons because we had people from all over the world and, for most of us,  half of the names are very difficult to pronounce.  For starters, different alphabets are used in different parts of the world and they are all represented in NYC.

if x=1, then lets use a new variable a to store the value of x, thus a=x which is now 1. Then as y=2, and x is empty, lets store the value of y to x, thus x=y. As y now is empty lets store the value of a to y which is y=a. Thus: a=x, x=y, y=a.
I'm confused by statement "x=y". If we assign initial values, this tells that 1=2. Or are there already variable values we say about, not initial?
seriously, i didnt get it. please help. im just a beginner . :(

The operator = (only one) doesn't mean "equal", it assign the value/expression of the right side of the statement to the variable of the left side. So, the statement "x=y;" means that the value of the y (in this case 2) is stored in the variable x.

There are two things you need to keep in mind to understand that process.

 1. the = symbol doesn't mean equal as it does in mathematics; it mean 'is assigned the value|expression. Some languages different symbols. And you should read x = 2 as 'to the variable x assign the value 2'.
 2. Variables in computing are also different for variables in mathematics; they just represent a location in memory. Think of it as a holding container where you can put something then latter change your mind and put something else in.

No es un fallo. En codeboard exigen que se use Main.java y class Main para que pueda ser ejecutado (es una condición de la plataforma).

java nombredeprograma.java
javac nombredeprograma

Lo debe hacer en el mismo directorio donde se encuentren sus ficheros.

También puede usar otras herramientas estilo codeboard que hay en internet.

children.body: "Special Methods of the Objects" -- In the videos the term Object is defined as an instance of the class. Constructors are presented as special methods of the Class...that are used to build the objects. The video talks about several constructors for the class "car" --- not for the object car.  Now...I guess the methods of the class become the behavior for instantiated objects...but I don't expect an object could construct itself...since...it doesn't exist until constructed?  Should that question read "special methods of a class"...or can we use objects and class interchangeably in this context?

Helps sort of... I get that "Constructors" are special methods.. I get that you can only call them once when you create the object...so my question revolved around the "Constructors are..." question in the exercise.   One of the correct answers is that Constructors are.... special methods of the objects. Would it not be more correct to say that constructors are special methods of the class. Seems nitpick...but object and class are not being used interchangeably...so I thought I'd ask.

Like Cookie_Jarvis, I was questing the validity of the answer to the following: 
"Constructors are:
**special methods of the objects**, used to create the objects, - correct"

Emphasis is mine. 

Literature so far provides that constructors are special methods of "class" and not of object. If objects were to change state, then mutating methods are implemented to tweak specific fields/attributes. Constructors play sort of midwifery role when creating an object and no more.

I have tried to instantiate an object using the constructor of its class and that gave an error. 

I think the question in question needs rephrasing for clarity.

Dear Cookie_Jarvis, motbat,

I understand that the answer "special methods of the objects" is not appropriate since constructors are used to create objects and are not used by objects. We have removed the ending "of the objects" of that sentence to avoid confusions.

Dear CarlosAH, 
Many thanks for your response.

A constructor is, to make it easy, a method thats always executed when declaring a class, like a the MAIN body of a class. If you dont declare it, the compiler will add an empty constructor but you wont see it, if u do, itll execute its code as you programmed it, like checking variables or using parameters passed to the class to initialize its own ones... Whatever you want to do. So yes, constructors are special methods cos they always get executed whether you want or not

Hi there,
I don't really see why the previous prey-predator value has to be stored in a different variable, since after going through the loop once, it's going to use the previous value anyway, it will just renew it (PreyPred[0] = PreyPred[0] *....) I compiled and ran it, and it works fine, getting the results that you have. Do I have to do it anyway just to get 2 points? The code will be more bulky and less efficient.
Thanks!

Bill - your explanation is good real world practice, but the specification given doesn't say what should happen to the original value of predPrey outside of the loop.  Different people will do different things (much like in the real world!).

The scoring rubic states:

> Inside the loop, the previous values of preyPred are stored in another
> variable, in order to be able of performing the calculation.

The absolute reason needed to store the value in a temporary variable is because if you don't you trash the values for the population calculation of the second species.

I won't be marking people down if the original values of predPrey are trampled upon simple because the scoring rubic / specification doesn't say the values should or shouldn't be stored outside of the loop.  It only specifies what is to happen *inside* the loop.
Nor would I mark people down for that reason.  I simply comment on best practice for the real world.
Ah, you're right. My bad!

Hey William,
thanks for such a solid reply, I was too quick to judge. It was my mistake. I'm just starting to learn and trying to be as efficient as I can, but at the beginning I'm prone to mistakes. But I did learn from your reply.

Regards,
Makar

title: Are the instructions for getSalary() correct?

children.body:
For the getSalary function, the assessment instructions say:

> The Employee class must implement the following Employed interface,
> which includes one single method double getSalary() that returns the
> total salary of the employee:
> 
> public interface Employed { double getSalary(); }

I might be totally missing it, but I am under the impression from the videos that interfaces should be in their own file and are implemented later by other classes. The instructions make it sound like the Employed interface should be inside the Employee class, which makes no sense to me.

The **Rubric** says something different:

> getSalary is declared as abstract and so the Employee class is also abstract.

This suggests that getSalary() should be an abstract method inside the Employee class rather than an interface as stated in the instructions.

I am guessing that the instructions for getSalary() are wrong. Perhaps a member of the staff could clarify.

Many thanks.

I am working under the assumption that getSalary() is an abstract method in the Employee class that gets implemented in the other three classes. I don't think it's supposed to be an interface. Everything makes sense that way, and it all works.

But I'll see if we get some clarification before I submit my answer.

Employed is interface that contains one method, called getSalary(). Employee implements the interface, but due to how exercise is given you cannot implement it fully in the Employee class. Under these circumstances, you have only one option - make getSalary() in Employee abstract and do the actual implementation in subclasses. But Employee **has to** implement the interface. In general you have to able to do this: Employed a = new *whatever subclass of Employee()*

I do it the other way around, since i wrote the getsalary method taking department as reference. I just wrote on the 3 diferent deparments a constructor to validate the final salary to be greater than 0 and the "implementation" of the interface method like the instructions says

I agree with recycledx: create the interface Employed, then class Employee implements it partially and should in consequence be abstract. Finally the abstract method getSalary is defined in the other classes

In other programming courses I've also seen little differences between rubric and instructions, so if you are in a hurry you can lose points easily.  What I usually do is to follow the instructions as best as I can, and then I revise my whole program reading the rubric carefully, making little changes to conform to it.

title: What about having the department in the Employee class ?

children.body:
I can't figure out why the department should be an attribute of the Employee class, as there is one subclass for each department ?

And if so, do we have to provide getters and setters for this attribute ? It might be confusing, if the department of an employee was to be set to another than the subclass he belongs to ??

I would rather define the department as a final string value in each of the subclasses...
Am I missing something ?
It is more efficient to define department once in superclass Employee than to define it for every subclass of Employee.

Suppose Anna becomes a manager and someone does that by setting her department to "Management".
The salary calculation will become false as the rules for it are in the subclass which will remain "Engineering"...
To avoid that problem, I decided to declare the setter setDepartment as private in the Employee class, so you can check the value when calling the constructor, but NOT change it afterwards.
Am I right ?
Great catch Catheryne.

Changing an existing employee's department without recreating the employee as an object of its new subclass would break the model. So, yes...in Catheryne's example you would have Anna as a manager but without the mandatory INDEFINITE contract and corresponding starting salary. That paradox would also apply to resetting a contract as they are mandated for managers and administrators. You can check in a setContract method for the department and vice versa for resetting a department. However, I don't see a way to handle the salary because Anna would still an object of the Management subclass regardless of her new attributes. For practical reasons though, if it's not in the rubric, it's not in my submitted assessment.

I don't see confusing if an employee change the department, simply you replace the old object for a new one. You could automatize the process overloading the constructor in the child classes, to construct a new employee of one type based in an old one of other type.

Hey || dosent work, and for&& we should also get an answer when nOne=6 and nTwo=48, i.e 288. But my last answer comes out to be 120.
Please help.
It is best to use || because we need to stop the loop when one of the two conditions no longer TRUE

I assumed the answer should be nTwo >= 50 since the question is:
    Add another condition so the program also stops when the value of nTwo variable is **greater than or equal to 50.**

I agree with JordiPM that you have to use the && and not the || because you want the loop to stop when any one of the conditions gets false and not when both get false at the same time. 

So in the step of the programm when it printed 120 (so result = 120, nOne=7, nTwo=96) the while condition checks result < 1000 (which is true) and n < 50 (which is false). true && false = false, so the while-loop stops working. If you used || instead: true || false = true, so the loop continues working.

And to answer your question, SadMaze, the condition n<50, that JordiPM used, is correct, because the while loop should STOP if n>=50, and after the while you define the conditions under which the loop continues working. It's in the verbal sense of "while": While this condition (n<50) holds true continue the loop, which means the same as stop the loop, if n<=50.

KristinHi can you clarify something? Are we not using || because that would mean that the loop will continue running even after it surpasses the nTwo<50 condition? And if instead we were to use &&, the loop will stop running when nTwo is less than 50. Thanks!

children.body:
Bribre:  Thank you for writing the empirical order growth, I was looking for these methods in how we calculate.

Can I ask you a question first before the rest?  
>Is the algorithm I presented above for arrays O(n) ?

<hr>

KHAN academy talked about Big Theta θ notation, and I have seen Big Omega<?> Ω notation but haven't had time to read up on either of them.  
<hr>
>Big O notation is not to determine a definitive answer about algorithm complexity.

Is there a notation that talks about algorithm complexity?

Also complexity is what is being talked about in the video: 
3.1 Counting Operations - Counting Operations @7:55
  

> "and say that the complexity of this algorithm is of the order of n,"

So is this then a bit misleading? If the real explanation is outside of the course focus I am ok with that, I am then just curious where I can continue to read about it until I fully understand.

Yes, I think what we are discussing here is outside the scope of this course. But it's an interesting, exciting and important topic. You will surely have to make further investigations to get things really clear. Or you might consider to take a course about algorithm analysis.... I think you already know the answer to your question?!
If the number of elements double, how will the number of operations change?
Take the ratio between the number of operations for doubled input and the number of operations for single input → The log₂ of this ratio is the exponent of N ;)


Concerning the main question:
First of all: Nothing is carved into the rock - So you might find different explanations.
Big O, big Omega, big Theta or Tilde notation are all about the complexity of algorithms. But big O is not a definitive answer. Commenly, these notations are used like this: - Tilde notation
→ provides the leading term (e.g. ~ 10 N²)
→ used to provide an approximtive model - Big Theta
→ provides the asymptotic order of growth (e.g. Θ(N<²))
→used to classify algorithms - Big Oh
→ provides Θ(N<²) and smaller (e.g. O(N²) ↔ **<=** N²)
→ used to develop upper bounds (worst case) - Big Omega
→ provides Θ(N<²) and larger (e.g. Ω(N²) ↔ **>=** N²)
→ used to develop lower bounds (best case) Don't hesitate to ask if you have more questions;)

> Taking a course on algorithm analysis.


I would like to do that.  Any suggestion on a course that has a good chance of answering these questions better?  Does EdX have one?

There are surely more than one course on the edX platform that treats analysis of algorithms in one way or another. Some go deeper, and other don't. IITBombayX offers a course "Algorithms" starting in September 2016 (C++).
What I can recommend are on the Coursera platform:
*Algorithms: Design and Analysis* from Tim Roughgarden/ Stanford - (language independent, means you can solve the assignments in Java, Python, C, C++ or whatever)
*Algorithms* from Robert Sedgewick and Kevin Wayne / Princton - (Java)
(When I took them, both didn't offer verified certificates, the first offered a statement of accomplishment and the second nothing at all, but was very rewarding. )

title: Helpful way to look at it

children.body:
Hey guys. This will be my first time helping anywhere in any forum/discussion ever on the internet. I just want to help anyone I can who is having any difficulty in this course. I know what it's like to feel stuck. My explanation is a bit long, but I think that if we use real world examples that most people can relate to, the solution is easier grasp. I only understand some of the very basics of programming because I have tried using C# in a popular game engine called Unity.

If you didn't complete the activity before this one called "Swapping Variables", I suggest trying it again until you do.

However, to make this current activity a little easier, just imagine that the "=" is actually an arrow like "<--". So, whatever is on the right of the arrow, is being put into the left side of the arrow. For example, when you see "a=x" you can say that you are putting x into a.

Let's say you have 3 buckets named "B", "G" and "A".

Bucket B contains green paint. 

Bucket G contains blue paint. 

Bucket A is empty.

We want to put the green paint into bucket G and the blue paint into bucket B, without mixing the paint.

So, since we have an empty bucket(A), we'll put the green paint from bucket B into bucket A for now, and wash out** bucket B so there's no paint left inside.

Then we'll put the blue paint from bucket G into bucket B(since it's now clean**) and wash out bucket G, so that we can now put the green paint(that we put in bucket A) into bucket G.

So there we have it... bucket B now contains the blue paint, and bucket G now contains the green paint.

What we did was:  A<--B, B<--G, G<--A  OR   a<--x, x<--y, y<--a    OR    a=x, x=y, y=a

**Note that in programming, we wouldn't actually be removing the value(paint in the example) from "x" when we put it in "a", but we will replace the value when we put the value of "y" into "x".

I really hope this helps.

to help, i thought of it like this as well:

a=x
x=y
y=a

x=y in the middle is what *transforms* (or the **change of state**) of the x from the FIRST assignment into y. 

hence, if you think "well i used to have x in the first statement, but i now have to consider that after the second statement, x is now y, of course i can say that instead of a=x, now y=a. )

title: can someone explain how we got 59 ?
source_category: Week 1 / 1.4 Expressions and Operations in Java (non graded activity)

children.body:
3+4+f*2 
since multiplication comes before addition and f=25 
we have 3+4+(25*2) = 7+50 = 57 
why the answer is 59 not 57 ?
how does x has the value of 26? it is decrement function and not increment
It is indeed a decrement function but through int d = c++;  d becomes 27 and c in the same time as well. For int f = --c; you need to place the new value of c (27) into the expression.

d=c++ , this will not assign 27 to d, since this statement assigns the value first , it actually assigns  current value c i.e. 26 to d and then c's value  will be incremented to 27.

So in the next statement when f uses c , the c has got a new increment value of 27 , then it will decremented to 26 and assigned to f since we have a prefix operator first(--) then the operand.

if there is a prefix operator , the value of the operand will be processed first and assigned to left hand operand. 
If there is a postfix operator, the current value of the operand will be assigned first then the operand will be processed.

You are right:   The provided code of the exercise will always return 0 if we call minIndex.   
The question is: Is this the correct behavior or is this one of the errors we have to find?    

The instructions for this assignment are:   
Operations with Arrays (II) (non-graded activity)

We implemented two methods, one for finding the index where the minimum of an array of integers is located, and another one to find the minimum value of the array. 

Compile and execute the provided code. As you can see, there are errors within these methods.
Please, find them and correct the code.

Hello XimoTur,   
Your solution works in most cases - but not in all.   

Principally, our method should work correctly for any legal input.   
An empty array is a legal input, and therefore, our solution should also work with an empty array as argument.   
That's in fact the reason for my response to the OP.

Another point to consider:   
The two methods contain in great parts the same code.     
This is not incorrect - but it's against the DRY principle (Don't Repeat Yourself)   

When we find that we are writing the same code again, this should make us think about «is this really necessary?» and «is there a possibility to do it better?»

In the given case, the methods are short - and code repetition is not a great problem here.    
But we can think about the possibility to call one of the methods as a helper function to the other.     



It doesn't make the code more efficient - we still will loop over the entire array for both, finding the min- value and finding the min-index.

I see that you were reasoning about «What if count is negative?» and this let you use Math.abs().   

But: Can it really be that count becomes negative in a well balanced string?    
Or is a negative value of count not a sign that our string is not balanced?

What do you get if you call depthSimple with a string like "(5))+(7 - (10)"  ?

ltrivas,      
Yes, this is a correct solution - Good work :-)
   
Your logic is straightforward: As soon as you come upon a closing parenthesis without having seen at least one corresponding opening parenthesis, you throw an error. 
After having scanned the entire string, you check if all opening parenthesis have been closed.

RonnellCanada,      
As I wrote above: It's never too late to post your code for discussion!    
Perhaps it would be better to start a new thread next time instead of commenting on an "old" one - where "old" is also meant as long...    
Discussing different things in one single thread can make the forum difficult to follow ;-)

That said, let's talk about your code:   

I'm sorry to tell you, but your solution doesn't work.        
There are two main reasons:    

1)  You set the variable max to half the length of the input string.  This will only be true for some rare corner cases.   

At this point it would be interesting to know your thoughts - it seems to me that you misunderstood what this variable max should represent: The depth - the grade of nesting.   
       
If there are no nested parenthesis, the depth is 1.   
Examples: `"(...)"` or `"(..)(..)"` or `"(..)(..)....(..)"`          
If there is at least one pair of parenthesis inside another pair of parenthesis, the depth is 2.     
Examples: `"((..))"` or `"((..)(..)...)"`       
If there is at least one pair of parenthesis inside another pair of parenthesis that is also inside another pair, the depth is 3.     
Examples: `"(((..)))"` or `"((..)(..(...))... )"`   
...and so on   

The length of the input string doesn't give us any information that could be used to deduce whatever about the parenthesis it might contain - because this string can (and most probably will) also contain other characters.      
Therefore, whether the length of the input string is odd or even doesn't play a role here.

We do not know anything about the characters the string contains. And our solution should work with all kind of strings:   
`"()"` or `"((a + b) - (c + d) * 27) / a * (b - c)"` or `"a + b + c"` or even `"hello world"` are all legal input strings.                     


 
2) When counting the parenthesis, your code only checks whether the number of opening and closing parenthesis match.   

It does not check if the

Intentaré Explicar como funciona.
Para empezar estamos de acuerdo que c=26
la posición de la variable y de "++" o "--" es importante por que indica el orden de los eventos.
En nuestro caso la instrucción 
int d = c++;
Asigna primero el valor de "c" a "d" y luego lo incrementa en uno; es decir "d" queda con el valor 26 y "c" se aumenta en uno, queda en 27
La siguiente instrucción
	int f = --c;
Primero se le resta uno a c ya que "--" está primero; Como el valor de c había sido aumentado en la instrucción anterior en uno y era de 27, ahora se le resta uno y queda en 26. Este valor es el que se asigna a "f" con lo cual la instrucción última
System.out.println(3+4+f*2);
Se resolverá dandole a "f" el valor de 26.

Espero haber podído aclarar algo el asunto.
Un saludo a todos.
ok, gracias por la aclaracion

Es simple, c++ es una forma corta de escribir c = c + 1, cuando asigno el valor a d queda d = c = c + 1, como hay dos asignaciones solo puede tomarse una y es la primera o sea d = c y descarta el = c + 1. En cambio si pones ++c seria igual a escribir c + 1 = c por lo que d toma el primer valor en la asignacion que es c + 1.

title: Conflicts of interest between stakeholders
source_category: Week 1 / 1.a.1. The rise of learning analytics in higher education - Part 1 - Quiz

children.body: It seems intuitive that policy should aim to support the needs of all stakeholders, and levels of interest. Is it inevitable that there will be some areas of conflict, e.g. between generating consistent data across teaching programmes versus allowing freedom for innovative pedagogy?

This was an interesting introduction and made me ponder on this thought: we cannot forget the Industry, i.e. employers, as powerful stakeholders here. 
Embedding employability skills in curriculum design and including competence-based assessment are initiatives naturally getting more and more traction in HE institutions. Likewise, HE students are more concerned as well with how to communicate the skills they gain. In this sense,  employers can be both: powerful sources and consumers of learning analytics. It makes sense to me that they are also considered when designing frameworks to standardise and define learning analytics that communicate this information.

...

Hi, I am not sure I understand the point made here - are you suggesting LA data is made available to employers? I agree its important to consider and embed employ ability outcomes within curriculum design where appropriate but suggest higher education goes above and beyond preparing someone for industry and employment - we are preparing students for a range of potential jobs and possible careers and to be flexible, critical thinkers and problem solvers.  I would suggest allowing employers access to LA about individual students may be shortsighted and problematic. Students may struggle with particular content for a range of reasons which might be out of their control - bad course deign, illness, lack of adequate or appropriate preparation - because the course does nor require pre-requisites.  They should not have to carry the weight of these things beyond institutional boundaries.

This is an interesting point. What processes would need to be in place to ensure the common understanding? Do we have suggestions as to how we engage stake holders (with differing interests) to reach a common understanding, thereby facilitate ownership, which would then allow them to engage better? I am keen to learn more.

Employers should have access to limited and specific "analyzed" data.  LA data can be used to contextualize the curriculum based on specific skill-set need(s)/overlaped with individual data and/or addressing areas of needed improvement through company/corporate training (one-time or ongoing).   Employers should have access to the internal data of its employees.  Externally, data from non-employees should only show up as filtered data made up of specifics recommendations put forth by a company for new hires or staff that has used external learning or training systems that can synthesize data  to make recommendations.
Agreed.

title: 'Adoption' is the key word here
source_category: Week 1 / 1.a.2. The rise of learning analytics in higher education - Part 2 - Quiz

children.body: There are some great examples of student-led projects using data and learning analytics techniques, as well as collaborative projects between academics and students. However, these tend to sit in the experimental end of the spectrum, with top down and bottom up approaches leading to adoption.
Very good comments. Do you know of examples where students are influencing the type of data they "produce and receive"?

There is much debate about considering student as customer - it can be a really problematic approach as a customer is not a partner - students are beginning to be seen more as partners in learning and as participants who can lead, take control of and manage their own learning. To see them as customers gives them little agency in the process of learning but frames them as consumers or recipients - opening the door to didactic teaching methods.

The LARC project at the University of Edinburgh is a participatory study, which encourages students to reflect critically on the automatic feedback generated by algorithms based on their VLE activities. https://larc-project.com/

The discussion around student led LA initiatives is interesting. On the one hand there seems to be one school of thought that students, as the least informed, have the least to give. On the other hand, no one questions enhanced search engines generating new sales leads (e.g., amazon). I'd be interested in spending some more time looking at student led initiatives to better understand what has been shown to be successful.

When considering students as customers, one is viewing the institution as profit-generating organization with defined goal of making money out of the customers, in this way students; instead of a non-profit educational institution where learning and equipping the mind are the primary goals to develop responsible citizens and future leaders.

Another option would be: `System.out.println( String.valueOf(b.charAt(0)) + c.charAt(0));`

The result is the same, but without the use of one of these options,
 you would get 149 (T = 84 and A = 65 in ASCII). Because with `charAt(0)` you get the ASCII-value of the character at the specified position.
So without clarifying that you want to handle these characters as a String you would just add the ASCII values.

title: incorrect?
source_category: Week 1 / 1.2 Precedence rules of operators (non graded activity)

children.body:
Hello,

8/4 * 2 should equal 1 (multiplication has precedence over division) yet the exercise accepts 4 (8/4) = 2 * 2 = 4 as the correct response. Am I missing something here?

Without parentheses you do division or multiplication first, depending on left-to-right.

In grade school I had a teacher that said Multiplication had higher priority than division, but he was wrong.

That's not correct:
 - Multiply, Divide and Modulus are at the same level.
 - Add and substract are at the same level but lower than previous one.

In the same level: left to right.

This is the same in most of the main programming languages. In the case of Java you may look at: https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/java/nutsandbolts/operators.html

(don't worry if you don't understand everything there, it's sooo exhaustive)

In any case: if you're in doubt use a pair of parenthesis. They don't harm your program and may even make it more readable.
These exercises are the best argument for using parenthesis!
Very helpful. I think this was clarified in the explanation given after the exercises.
i have used the calculator and divide 5 by 6 the result was 0.8333 and because no decimal part in integer so i think the result = 0

This is true. % is the modulo division and and not the normal division /.
n % m is the remainder after the euclidean division of n by m.
if n=m*q+r then n%m=r with r=0,1,...,m-1. Here m=6 so n=6*q+r; r=0 or 1 or 2 or 3 or 4 or 5. Here q=0; So 5=6*0+5
This means that if r=0 then n is a multiple of m otherwise it is not.

Thank You , your answer guide me to searching over the net ( no previous info about remainder ) and i have found this example:
A remainder is the amount left after a number is taken out of another number a set amount of times, and Since 5 is less than 6 and no number to be taken out , then 5 it is the remainder.

" . . . example: A remainder is the amount left after a number is taken out of another number a set amount of times, and Since 5 is less than 6 and no number to be taken out , then 5 it is the remainder."  

That is called making up a solution to fit a problem!  5 is NOT divisible by 6 using whole numbers.  The answer is 0 (zero)!

Correction! The "/" does **NOT** represent integer division, it represents division. However, in Java, C, C++ and several other languages, an integer divided by an integer results in integer division. If you change one or both of the values to a double, then the result will be a double.

The question did not ask "is valid".

it asked "which is the comparison needed".

">=" is not a mistake, but if we won't check this - answer also must be correct. We have already know that there is no repeated values in the array, so why we should use ">=", ">" is more than enough...

IriaEA:  I would add that the ">" operator will give you the first value in the array that is the largest value and the ">=" operator will give you the last value in the array that is the largest value when we have an array with duplicate numbers.  And since the question only asks you to find the greatest value it would give you an index with the greatest value by using > or >= in an array with multiple greatest values as well.

I would argue that an array with multiple greatest values such as "int array[] = {1,23,14,205,48,205};" would have two acceptable solutions 3 or 5 for the index of the largest value.  They would both answer the question, "What is the largest value in the array?"

Niccolo: I did not care for the question personally at first because I felt that ">" was the right answer.  But it caused me to think more about the code and now I am ok with both answers.

I have been running code snippets in the java environment as these questions are like home work or in class work and by running the code segments we can also determine what valid scenarios exist.  And this can then allow us to ask the questions "why does >= work as well for the given example?" and "What does it do in other scenarios?"

IMHO, you always should use a loop when you can, instead of recursion. It is more efficient from the standpoint of speed and resource consumption. And it becomes more noticeable as the number of iterations grows.

Recursion should be used when you have not idea (or it's very hard to know) about how many iterations you'll have.

title: lost in the maze
source_category: Week 3 / Lab-3 Modeling maze problem and maze solution

children.body: This section has be really confused. Can anyone explain the array system used here. I have a complete mental block! (pun intended)

Ive juts realised after looking at the next tab that this was wrong. The maze array is in rows from the picture.
So imagine a row 1 on the maze show and move right one space at a time, you get obstacle, obstacle,open space,open space,open space,open space,open space,open space
So the first row of the array corresponds with 0 0 1 1 1 1 1 1
The second row is where the maze is starting and so the first number is 2 etc.

m2igm you are right. 

The picture represents a board with 8 rows and 8 columns. The array called "maze problem" represents the distribution of elements in the maze. If you change each number using a piece of drawing you would reproduce the picture. Let me show you how:

0 represents an obstacle (a green area in the picture)
1 represents a position available (a piece of the path in the picture)
2 represents the initial position (the character that will traverse the maze)
3 represents the goal (the candy).

In the second case, the array called initial state we  print the maze with symbols that are more visual than numbers.

The # represents an obstacle
The. It represents a position available, or a piece of the path.
The + represents the character running through the maze (it's like his footprint).
The * represents the final goal (the candy).

In the third case, the array called final state represents the maze once it has been solved. You can see how our character represented by the "+" symbol has left his footprints in the form of "+" from its initial position through the maze to arrive to the final position (the candy)

You are wrong Niccolo. You can perform the first calculation, say hares without storing the lynx previous population because you are not modifying the lynx value in this calculation. You must store the previous population of hares before this first calculation though so the value is still available for the second calculation on the lynx. The second calculation simply takes the current value of lynx population, which hasn't been modified yet, and performs the formula with the previously stored value of hares. Works perfectly and is the most efficient.

In any code everybody should also think about how difficult particular code construct is to understand and how long takes it to get into it. Technically you can work with only one temp variable, but for me code is much cleaner and first-sight-understandable with two.

For me demanding such things in rubric is also little bit too much, but also take in mind that code could be potentially reviewed by somebody who just started learning programming and such shorts might be difficult to get into.

Does file.listFiles() method return files AND **directories**?

From the documentation:

**listFiles()**
Returns an array of abstract pathnames denoting the **files** in the directory denoted by this abstract pathname.

**list()**
Returns an array of strings naming the **files and directories** in the directory denoted by this abstract pathname.
Do you mean the documentation "is lying"?

Hi,

You forgot reading the rest of the documentation:
https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/io/File.html#listFiles--

>Returns an array of abstract pathnames denoting the files in the directory denoted by this abstract pathname.

>If this abstract pathname does not denote a directory, then this method returns null. Otherwise **an array of File objects is returned, one for each file or directory** in the directory. **Pathnames denoting the directory itself and the directory's parent directory are not included** in the result.

Question here: does one of the proposed answers suggests that the method `foo()` prints **all** the files and directories, including the parents directories???????????

I got one attempt wrong, and I would not like to lose points because of some kind of tricky formulation.

Actually I didn't forget reading the rest of the (detailed) documentation. I simply did not read it. Why? You'd better direct me to reading the source code of the method )))) I believe reading summary description should be enough. For example let's have a look at the summary description of a similar method list() where it's cleary said "files and directories". I believe it's a bad practice for the authors of the course to use such unfair tricks. We're learning programming and not documentation investigation )))

Hi,

Well, there are in fact a couple of tricky things here. The first one is that searching the documentation is an important part of programming in real life. And it's really important to develop the skills of being able to manage to find the information you need (local documentation, Internet, colleagues, wherever). By the way, the information you need may be technical and also non-technical, but that's another story.

Another tricky thing here is the fact that, if I'm not wrong, Java has a background in Unix systems (Solaris operating system is a Unix system). And in Unix, everything is a file. A directory is also a file... So sometimes, it is not always clear for me that when someone talks about '*files*' he means '*files and directories*' or just '*files*'...

Another problem for me, as I was saying in my previous message, is that I don't know whether the instructors consider the directories '`.`' and '`..`' as part of what they call '*all the files and directories*'. So I don't know what to answer to this question (I only have one submission left).

I personally think that the instructors should give hints, not only because some things are a somewhat ambiguous, but also because this course is aimed at beginners.

I am not an expert on computer programming, but I know enough to be disturbed by the lack of professionalism in this course, both in the presentation of the material and the wording of the exam questions. I understand that I am not the only one having difficulty interpreting the meaning of several of the questions, not to mention the inconsistencies and grammatical errors that are scattered throughout the material. The team that created this course is obviously talented in Java, but the overall design of the course is flawed. I hope that more effort will be put into communicating clearly to the students in the future.

-A Student

title: Has anyone successfully compiled and run project?

children.body:
I have had to give a java extension to all of the files I created in order to test the program. When I did that, the compiler required the Employed interface to be in a separate java file, after which I got an error message that getSalary() in Employee cannot implement getSalary() in Employed, even though I declared that Employee implements Employed. The error message appears in all of the extensions of Employee.

Any clues on what I'm doing wrong?

@DianaCook

Yes, all file names must be exactly the same name as their class names!

I implemented getSalary() in each of subclasses of Employee and whole project compiled successfully. It should have return double.

I call it from the array (in Staff main) such as "staff[0].getSalary()" through the loop.

However, I did not put the signature of getSalary(), with empty body, in Employee class but still works. Am try to sort out if it is correct.

Hi, Employee is an abstract class and getSalary() in Employee is also an abstract method, leave the body empty.

So the header of Employee class should be:
public abstract class Employee implements Employed

and the signature of the getSalary() method is:
public abstract double getSalary();

In every child class you have to implement getSalary().
The thing is java is case sensitive, so one different letter and itll be completely different

Thanks to your responses,jhon_mass and marigold088. I did not want to spend a lot more time on this assignment and submitted it when I got it to produce the desired output.

I just declared the Employee class to be an abstract class and included abstract double getSalary(); in the code.

Yes, I know that - thanks anyway. I've presented the first peer assessment, I've revised a load of works (more than the ones required). But it doesn't make sense to publish a due date in the left column for the end of the assessment period, and not for the due date of the presentation.

"But it doesn't make sense to publish a due date in the left column for jcoppens said: "It doesn't make sense to publish a due date in the left column for the end of the assessment period, and not for the due date of the presentation."

I strongly agree.  I now realise I have 11 hours to prepare and submit peer assessment 2 for review!!

It would make much more sense to display the EARLIER date.  I thought I had a week to do the programming; now realise I have 11 hours.

It shouldn't be necessary to read the threads. We should be able to rely on the summary information provided.  Most of us have jobs etc. & are obliged to adopt a "Just in Time" approach to the course.

I have successfully passed all Exams from week 1 to 5 

And i have scored 8/10 on the first peer assesment. 

My total percentage is 64 

if i am unable do my peer assesment 2 , will i still get my certificate?
I think that the percentages are presented cumulatively.

I agree with 3gfeathers. The graded tests account for 75% of the total score; P2P1 10%; and P2P2 15%. So, if one gets an average of 80% in the weekly exams, one will have a total score of 60%, the passing mark. Grades from the P2P assignments will not be necessary to pass. On the other hand, the P2P assignments are great learning experiences.

Dear learners,

To get a certificate it is not compulsory to do all the activities, but it is necessary to go over 60% of the final grade. In order to know your progress, just click on the tab "Progress": https://courses.edx.org/courses/UC3Mx/IT.1.1x/1T2015/progress.

If you need to check the grading policy, just have a look at the syllabus: https://courses.edx.org/c4x/UC3Mx/IT.1.1x/asset/IT.1x-Syllabus.pdf

Regards,

*Java MOOC* Team

title: Helping each other
source_category: Week 1 / 1.4 Play with the ternary operator (non graded activity)

children.body:
As others said (please read their explanations) think of it as IF statements for lazy persons:
For those with doubts please see 2 examples from the table:
a=false b=false
	b?true:false
(b is false so we get the part after the ":" -> false)
	b?false:true
(b is false so we get the part after the ":" -> true)
	b?a:true
(b is false so we get the part after the ":" -> true)
	b?a:false
(b is false so we get the part after the ":" -> false 

a=true b=true
	b?true:false
(b is true so we get the part before the ":" -> true)
	b?false:true
(b is true so we get the part before the ":" -> false)
	b?a:true
(b is true so we get the part before the ":" -> "a" which is defined as true)
	b?a:false
(b is true so we get the part before the ":" -> "a" which is defined as true)

a=false b=true
	b?true:false
(b is true so we get the part before the ":" -> true)
	b?false:true
(b is true so we get the part before the ":" -> false)
	b?a:true
(b is true so we get the part before the ":" -> "a" which is defined as false)
	b?a:false
(b is true so we get the part before the ":" -> "a" which is defined as false)

Hope that helps everyone!!
Regards
Fsnog

title: Por qué el resultado es 1 si i no es menor que cero?

Hello josseluism,

Your explanation is not completely right.

If i is 0 then it is not less than 0, then the if is false. But you have the empty statement. That is, you are saying the if is false, but you have a ';' following it, then you are doing nothing in that if. So the program will execute the next line as it has jump over the if. The if ends at the first ';'.

Regards,

Lucía.

title: Why should there be a condition i<=num/2
source_category: Week 2 / 2.1 Translation between while and for (non graded activity)

children.body: Hi ! I don't understand why do we have to confine the divisor integers to half of the divident?! We can instead use the condition i <= n to get all divisors ( the numbers that that can be divided with the original number).
I guess he/she was asking if we consider 1 as a possible answer why  couldn't the subject number divide itself as a possible answer.

Hi Lucia! I got your point. But by this method we are losing one of the divisors, which is number itself. E.g - if 20 is the divident you won't get 20 as divisor (but 20 is actually a divisor of 20 itself) in this method because the divisors get confined to half of the divident number (i.e 10).

Hello pnitish,

You have a point there, but for time reasons is better to add, if it is necessary, the number itself at the end and do it with `num/2` as you are saving half of the time it will cost the same operation but with `num` only.

Regards,

Lucía.

title: Error in Answer to The Minimum Number

children.body: The loop invariant proposed for the correct answer ignores the value a[0]. It should be a[0...k] because a[0] could be smallest value in the array.
I'm confused.. so  min == min(a[1...k]) is still the correct answer?

Dear bmgtenty:

Yes! The question is about loop invariant instead of class invariant.

Regards,

Florina

I'm afraid it is wrong. The correct loop invariant should be min == min(a[0..(k-1)]).
See Wiki's page about an example of a loop invariant for finding max:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Loop_invariant#Informal_example

With over 30 years programming experience, I'm concerning about the quality of this course. For the fuel() example, the postcondition !empty() is bad - it doesn't tell us anything really useful. A better one should be fuel = amount + fuel' (where fuel' is the fuel before change). See this page for better explanation:
https://www.cs.umd.edu/class/fall2002/cmsc214/Projects/P1/proj1.contract.html

Everyone knows we have to eat healthily. The important thing is the correct way. Providing bad examples don't help and even hurt you. For example, avoid animal fats and use vegetable fats instead. Is it really true? Read this:
http://www.redorbit.com/news/health/1112778845/animal-fats-better-than-vegetable-fats-020613/

More importantly, anyone told you how the vegetable oils are produced? Chemical? Refined? High temperature? GM?

title: Sum of Natural Numbers in Video Improving Efficiency

children.body:
Hi,
In the video for Improving efficiency the formula for the sum of the natural numbers is n*(n-1)/2
But testing that and searching shows that the formula is n*(n+1) / 2.

Is there a reason for it being n*(n-1)/2 in the video that I have missed?

Thanks

I was on the other video.

I had also trouble understanding this exercise but I got it after reading this answer: from **Carlamarrow**
Hope this doesn't confuse you further, but you could think of this in terms of objects to understand the concepts of 'assigned variable', 'empty variable', 'result' is always on the left and what is to be evaluated is on the right of the operator (you execute what is on the right of the equation and put the results on the left). So, if you had a 'cookie on a plate' (x) and an 'orange in a bowl' (y) and an empty bag (a). How would you put the cookie in the bowl and the orange on the plate if you can only move one item at a time and only one item could be in/on a container at a time?

Just to add a simple tip. The point of this exercise is that you can't swap two values quickly without losing the data from one of the variables (x or y). You need the (a) variable to temporarily store a value while you do the swap. You will comes across this sort of log a lot when you start programming.

Hello Guys,

This is very easy program we need to create a sequence in three steps where x and y value should be swap so we need to do step in our copy first like:
if you have x=1; and y=2; and you need to swap these two values in 3 steps please see below and do in the copy you will definitely understand;

a=x; (value of x will go into a and now x is empty)
x=y ; (now y which is convert into x so x is 2 here)
y=a; (now a which is 1 convert into y)
so by writing these three steps you can easily swap the values.

Thanks,
Absar Ahmed.

title: Order of Presedence
source_category: Week 1 / 1.2 Precedence rules of operators (non graded activity)

children.body: I always followed the Rule BOMDAS, B being parenthesis , *, / then + and lastly -. Is it correct that in Java Subtraction has precedence over Addition as seen in the notes for this topic ?
Division and multiplication, like addition and subtraction hold the same precedence, and are solved in order of appearance in the equation.
Same here - what I learnt was PEMDAS - parens, exponents, mults, div, add, suntract. The answer to 8/4*2 is 8/6 or 1.333' ... humphhh!

Actually, more correctly it is Pe(MD)(AS) or Bo(MD)(AS) showing true precedence.

At equal levels of precedence, operations are carried out in order from left to right.

So 8/4*2 has two operations, both with the same precedence. We will carry them out in order from left to right. Using grouping symbols to show this, we could right 

(8/4)*2 = 2*2 = 4. 

It isn't that we have given division precedence over multiplication, but rather that since multiplication and division have the same precedence, we have carried them out from left to right.

The same rule applies when we evaluate

8*4/2 = (8*4)/2 = 32/2 = 16.

Multiplication isn't taking precedence over division, it just happens to come first (reading from left to right) so is carried out first.

children.body:
Dear all,

For those who does not know this riddle, I offer a (digital) beer to the one who solve it. It is in line with this video:

The director of a prison take a prisoner to an empty room with 2 doors on other side and a guard on front of each door. One door is named "A" and the other "B".

The director explains the rules:
- One of the 2 guards lies systematically, while the other always says the true. They are a bit dumb and can only say "yes" or "no".
- One of the door give access to the street and liberty, the other drive straight to the scaffold and death.
- The prisoner can only make one question to one of the guard and must then make his decision of which door he will go through.

What is the right question?
You dont really know the one who say the truth from the one who tells lie, so how do you know who to approach

Hi NuriaGC, i do not understand why will not be able to use the String Hello! again? Could you explain it, please? Or anyone?

Hi!

The main different between both of them is:

 - Return is used when we want to state the outcome of a method. In this case we're saying that the resulto of calling the sayHello() method is the String "Hello" and this result is store in memory. So that, if you need that String in any other part of the code you can have it by calling to that method.
 - System.out.println() it's only useful when we want to show something in console, but only in an informative way.

I hope you can understand it better with this explanation.

Best regards,

Nuria

title: Disagree with answer to answer(s) to Arguments and return values question
source_category: Week 3 / 3.1 Arguments and return values (non graded activity)

children.body:
I disagree with the first "correct" answer. 
Although it has not been implicitly covered in the course so far, we have covered arrays and a method can return an array which can contain multiple values!
had a same issue - I think the statement was a bit misleading
a function, by definition returns one value. an array IS the result returned, not its contents

The most correct definition would be that a method can return at most one **object**, so whether it is a group of numbers (an array) or a chain of letters (a string), at the end of the day we are talking about a sole object.

There was a lack of academic precision with it, for sure!
When you return an array, what you do is return a pointer to that array. So, when returning something, you will return at most a value(in case of arrays, a pointer).

Thanks, Draschler for the presentation. I think ethics is far one of the biggest stumbling blocks for LA in Europe currently. In your DELICATE list, you mention consent as a necessary step.  Of course, I agree. However, in cases where some students decide to opt-out, how can researchers deal with this especially in learning situations. How simple is it to remove certain students' logs from analysis?

Also, is it possible not to seek consent if my study, for example, requires no intervention with students? I mean, in case my intention is to work with teachers to use LA to improve course design without involving students. 

What do you think folks?
GDPR states you cannot collect data for one purpose and reuse the data for another one (unless the data contains no personal information anymore).

I have a question on the **'tracking' definition**. "the right to stop data tracking and still participate in the education processes". This is my current split: 
LA tracing is :  counting clicks, time spend per chapter, watch videos , number of interactions on a forum ...
Basis educational transactions in a learning management system: handing in a paper in time,  received grades, having participated on a forum.
The education process cannot continue without the last transactions, therefore consent is not needed to do so.
What is your opinion?

Hi there,

I would suggest there is also a major difference between learning and teaching use of data and research use. What I mean here is - I might consent to have myself and my data tracked to improve my learning and to allow my tutors to improve their teaching but not be happy with my data used for research purposes. So there are a few issues to consider - as MadAubergine points out we consent to collect and consent to use and then there is consent in relation to collection and use.  Its really complex.
Glad that it is meaningful to you.

I recognized that I signed up for the course thinking that I was going to learn how to work with some LA tools and, initially, I felt a little bit disappointed about "theoretical" aspect of the course. But I must say that the course has helped me to delve into many aspects that I would never have thought I would have to consider when implementing a LA project. I totally agree with Pedro's comment: "The SHEILA framework tries to help in the process of builiding with LA policy, pointing out important aspects to take into account."

I agree with @arruarte's comment about the initial "disappointment around the "theoretical" aspect of the course" as i myself had a very limited understanding of Learning Analytics and its uses (this is my first time working in an education institution). I had hoped that i would learn more about LA tools and how to use them, however, this course has definitely helped me gain a better understanding on  the actual implementation process of the LA policy and has covered some important points such as ethics and privacy (things that i had not considered before).

It happened to me in a similar way, I did not think there was such a solid theoretical foundation, however, thanks to the course I considered aspects that I would not have thought about when trying to implement data analysis in a higher education institution.

Undoubtedly, the benefits that can be obtained with the analysis of data will depend on the objectives defined by each institution and the decisions regarding the data that will be obtained and the use of them, but they should always be guaranteed to lead to an improvement in the teaching-learning process.

Krissia G.
I agree with the comments above and think that the Sheila Framework represents a very valuable approach to developing any new policy (and then effecting change) in higher education.

title: Draft doc and reflection on using the SHEILA framework

children.body:
My institution, a small private online college specialising in teacher education, wants to implement a LA policy and I’ve contributed [this draft][1] as a starting point. I found the SHEILA framework easy to use, although I sometimes found the Policy, Challenge, Action headings a bit unintuitive, i.e. it wasn’t always clear where some of my points belonged in this schema. This wasn’t insurmountable, though, and having to think about the issues in a structured way was a very useful exercise. The emphasis on stakeholders was particularly helpful and valuable, as this is going to be a major area of work; doing my own ad-hoc LA projects in my own little silo meant this was not something I’d previously given much thought to. Going through each component of the framework prompted me to think of many questions and considerations and look at them from different stakeholder perspectives. By the time I’d finished I realised just how much work is going to be involved in creating this strategy and how many questions will require answers.

My next step is to bring this draft to our LA strategy working group for their input. It’ll also be important to identify where procedures (existing and yet to be created) fit into the policy, and how the policy will sit with the overall policy and strategy landscape of the college. It is my hope that the comprehensive scope of the policy will help to inspire confidence in LA among institutional stakeholders and encourage engagement with, and the provision of resources for, the use of LA on a college-wide basis.

Given that the format of the SHEILA framework is reasonably flexible and customisable as is, I would not change it.

*1. In your view, how can the involvement of different stakeholders scale up the adoption of learning analytics and increase its sustainability?*
A learning analytics policy must be institution-wide; otherwise work will take place in a disjointed and ad-hoc way, which might be how a lot of LA initiatives begin but which

I really appreciate this insight of yours insight:

> Going through each component of the framework prompted me to think of many questions and considerations and look at them from different stakeholder perspectives

Identifying and involving stakeholders is one thing, but actually running through the 6 dimension of the framework from the perspective of each stakeholder is something I hadn't considered. Quite valuable, if only to help get buy-in from everyone.

You mention:

> It is my hope that the comprehensive scope of the policy will help to inspire confidence in LA among institutional stakeholders and encourage engagement with, and the provision of resources for, the use of LA on a college-wide basis.

One of the things I like about the SHEILA framework and the format we're using for this exercise is that it can be kept relatively light-weight. Do you have any strategies towards (or experience with) finding a balance between a large scope with lots of stakeholders and a large organisation versus keeping the working group nimble and flexible and not bogged down  by institutional politics?

From your policy doc it feels like the main goals of implementing LA is to improve the pedagogical / educational aspect (helping students and teachers). Business considerations are a distant second. Would you say this is indeed the case, or do you happen to be less interested / less well-versed in the business side, and would someone else add more business-related considerations to the dimensions? I'm curious to understand whether educational institutions who consider comprehensive LA solutions do so primarily driven by pedagogical or business...

smg36111, thank you for your comments. I hope there will be support for a LA initiative in my institution, although it's early days and the business case still needs to be made. One problem we may face is staff resources, as those of us who have any LA knowledge are stretched in different directions in our roles already and would need help to get started. No doubt this is a common problem.

jpoesen, thanks for your comments also. It's interesting that you mention institutional politics because this is one thing I think will be a challenge, even in a small working group. There is a difference in culture and in priorities between the 'academic' side and the 'support staff' side, especially with regard to technology, and a lot of the time we are not speaking the same language at all. I hope that the SHEILA framework will help tease out this relationship and help us to reach some unity of purpose. Re. your business/pedagogy question, I'm probably guilty of neglecting the business side as pedagogy is my area of interest :-) Also, any institutional moving/shaking in LA so far has been from the pedagogical side, e.g. small research projects using VLE data - there hasn't been much engagement with LA from the business side just yet. That is changing, however ... mostly thanks to GDPR, which has focused the minds of executive management on what we should be doing with all this data and, crucially, how not to fall foul of the new regulations while doing it.

Hi Julia, Being a small college will surely have some advantages for communication and implementation. Of course, our resources are also small so we will need to be realistic about the scope of what we can do. We are still at the stage of pulling together a LA working group and I hope my policy draft will be useful for giving us some direction. Thanks for your comment! Irene

Yes, ethics is a huge area of LA that needs more attention. It is one aspect that I hope the SHEILA framework can help my institution with, because we really need a policy that addresses this. I wish you the best of luck with your work!
