title: what is "false" and "true"?
source_category: Week 1 / Want-1 Leap year (non graded activity)

children.body: In the excercise description I'm missing the assignment of TRUE and FALSE to "leap year" vs "common year".

Thx, I would just like to have the instruction clearer. You say "We are asked a question: Is year Y a leap year?". Just add this question to the instruction and no more answers :)
Or did I get something wrong?

Hi,

Well, I think that this is the tricky part when it comes to programming: formulate the problem. Here, the instructions just say:

    > The algoritm to know if a given year is a leap year:

So, I guess we are supposed to understand that the question is to know "if a given year is a leap year", hence the question "is year Y a leap year?". We know there are just two alternatives: a year can be a leap year or not, i.e. true (leap) or false (not leap).

The pseudocode tries to confuse us by talking about "common year" and "leap year". And here, we are supposed to understand that "common year" is equivalent to "NOT leap year". At the end, we could rewrite the pseudocode using "it is NOT a leap year" and "it is a leap year". And further, we could rewrite the pseudocode using "return false" and "return true".

As I was saying at the beginning, one of the most important things in programming is to formulate the problem: what is the problem, what do we want to do, etc. It's necessary in order to come up with what they call a "well defined problem".

To sum up, I think that the instructors are encouraging us to read very carefully the instructions in order to extract every bit of useful information...

Would it kill anyone writing this question to just say "How do we determine if year Y is a leap year?" You're basically left to guess if a leap year or a common year is a true or false state. Poor time for ambiguity.

Question says variable x is not between integers 10 and 50, **including both**

Result says correct option is 50 < x || !(x > 9) and 50 < x || x < 10 

In both the above cases **50 is excluded**. I am unable to understand why 50 <= x || x <= 10 is wrong which is actually including both 50 and 10.

but || symbol is or, which means if the value on first condition is met it returns true and not checking on the second condition. i think it should use && symbol if i'm not mistaken. i don't know maybe my comprehension is not to deep on this subject.

If the first condition is true (for example, if **x** is 54), Billy, it means that **x** is *already* out of that interval. So there's no need to check the other condition too. This is the so-called "short-circuiting", meaning that the "circuit" composed of the conditions one writes is closed "short" - before its full length - because of how Java behaves. And the great majority of the times, it's a good thing that it does behave like this as it saves time, operating power and prevents many errors.

Now, if the first condition is *not* met (for example, if **x** is 6 and thus is smaller than 50), the second condition will be checked. And since 6 *is* smaller than 10, the partial result - the one for the right side of the || - will be **true**. And, since OR operator results in true *always except when both its operands are false*, this OR will *also* result true, since the right operand, as we said, is true.
I think the answer shown here is wrong. It should be 50 <= x || x <= 10
Just didn't follow what the question was trying to ask. I simply entered the highest and the lowest (negative) number possible overlooking the 5 bits.  I guess my mistake.

No. If you have a signed binary number of any size, the high order bit is always used as the sign bit, if it is 0, the number is a positive number, if it is 1, the number is negative. That leaves the rest of the bits to contain the actual value.
The question should have started with "IF" since the intent was to truly utilize only 5 bits of a byte .... the way it was written made it look like it was a typo error.

Hi,

Well, there are two programs

- `javac.exe`: the compiler
- `java.exe`: the interpreter

First, you have to compile the program:

    javac MyProgram.java
If all goes well, this creates a file named `MyProgram.class`

Second, you run the program:

    java MyProgram

(Notice: `MyProgram` without extension)

If you installed the JDK correctly, you *must* have a '`...\jdk1.8.0\bin`' folder somewhere. Notice: '`jdk...`', not '`jre...`'. And `javac.exe` must be there.

title: Is this a correct solution?
source_category: Week 3 / Lab-3 Looking for available positions

children.body: Yes. Add an negation for the result.  result = !( . . . . .)
Or.. return !result;

The method isAvailablePosition is already implemented for you. We only want you to test that it is working as expected. 

So, you only have to add new statements to the main method like this one:
System.out.println("Is (0,0) available?: " + isAvailablePosition(0,0));

Then you look at the array called maze. 

*The position (0,0) corresponds to row=0 and column=0. If you look this position at the array called maze contains a value 0, ie. maze[0][0]=0, and therefore is not a valid position for our character because 0 represents an obstacle, and then the method should return false, because position (0,0) is not available (contains a green zone in our blockly picture).

The position (10,0) is out of bounds because our maze has only 8 rows and 8 columns and therefore the method isAvailablePosition(10,0) should return false.

The position (0,2) is a valid position because maze[0][2]=1 and 1 represents a piece of path. 

We want you to add new senteces in the main method for example:
System.out.println("Is (4,5) available?: " + isAvailablePosition(4,5));

And then run the program and check the result. In this case the result should be true because maze[4][5] = 3 that represent the candy, that it is an available position for our character. 

Hope that helps, 

Greetings, 

Carmen

title: Why if (number <= 1)
source_category: Week 3 / Recap-3 Fix a Non-terminating Program (solution)

children.body:
Why `if (number <= 1)`? This will mean that the factorial of a negative number will be 1, but if you try to get the factorial of a negative number on a calculator you will get an error.

I understand that if the condition were `if (number == 0)` you would get a stackoverflow exception, but then surely the best solution would be to check for negative values and deal with them accordingly?
We can also test if number <1 and print "Does not define"

Hi,

I guess these are the situations where you would throw an exception such as `IllegalArgumentException` or similar, literally saying to the caller that the argument is illegal for this function.

Another possibility seems to be to return a special value `NaN` (Not a Number, or `Double.NaN`), like function `Math.sqrt()` does when the argument is negative: https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/lang/Math.html#sqrt-double-.

A quick-and-dirty solution would be to return `0` or a negative value, such as `-1` because we know that the factorial of a number cannot be less than `1` (`0! = 1`). Thus, receiving this kind of value as the result tells us that something is wrong.

And a more general solution would be to avoid calling the function with an illegal value, by validating the user input, or by preventing other calculations to produce an illegal argument for the function.

Anyway, I think that the function MUST test the value of the argument passed in, and not rely on the "we know that it won't happen" or "we hope it won't happen" kind of opinion, because it CAN happen and likely WILL happen: the users always find a way to crash your software :-), and the programmers have to think ahead.

BTW, I heard the "we know it won't happen" and the "we hope it won't happen" things in my workplaces... Another version is "during the training sessions, we will tell the users not to do it"... And yep, you're thinking right: eventually a user did it...

Hi,

@Tribble

It works fine when you call `fact(0)`, or `fact(1)`, or `fact(100)`, etc. But what happens if you call `fact(-1)` or, more generally, if you call `fact(...)` with a negative parameter?

We know that the function `factorial` is not defined for negative numbers, but the computer does not know about that. You have to tell it to do something when the argument is mathematically incorrect. Some possible options: consider that a negative parameter is the same as 0 and just return 1, or throw an exception, or return a zero or negative value that you can test and see that something went wrong, because we also know that the factorial of a number is always positive.

*A normal class may have abstract methods, that is, methods that are declared but not yet implemented (§8.4.3.1), only if it is an abstract class. **It is a compile-time error if a normal class that is not abstract has an abstract method.** A class C has abstract methods if either of the following is true:
• Any of the member methods (§8.2) of C - either declared or inherited - is abstract.
• Any of C's superclasses has an abstract method declared with package access, and there exists no method that overrides the abstract method from C or from a superclass of C.*

title: Why is it required to store previous values of preyPred in variables, when variables could store new values instead, using preyPred as old values for calculation before updating preyPred to new values?

children.body: kwb, if you want to access those variables again if you decide to do something else with preyPred[], those original values are lost. For example, if you want to modify the array values for whatever reason in the future if you want to improve on the program. It is good form to store original preyPred[] values in a variable, as you never know if you need to access them again. Although your code does work, it is not good form. Read the specifications before submitting code!

I agree that its good form to never modify values of a parameter passed as a reference (pointer) unless that is the purpose of the functionality.

However the RUBRIC doesn't care about this, its making sure that you are not tainting the values of the calculation by ensuring that at least 1 extra variable is used to store the values.  This is the only reason I can see for why we specifically store the values "inside" the loop.  If I were getting the values sent from the array to use they could be stored before using them in the class independent of the loop.  And I would expect language such as "ensure the original values in the array are preserved".  

**RUBRIC: "Inside the loop, the previous values of preyPred are stored in another variable, in order *to be able of performing the calculation*."**

title: What went wrong with Week 3 Peer Assessment?

children.body:
I've taken part in many courses over the last couple of years of which a fair number used peer assessment. This is the first time that I have come across students failing to get *any* peer reviews. Although peer assessments are more common (and better handled) on Coursera, I have taken edX courses using them but without seeing such adverse comments--so there should be nothing wrong with the basic mechanism.

The question is why so many of those who submitted assignments failed to review their peers. I think there are probably two elements to this. Firstly, there are a proportion of students dropping out each week. From the figures I have seen this seems to average out at around 20% (of the remaining active body) each week and/or assessment activity. So we would expect to see fewer than five reviews per peer on average. Secondly, the edX peer assessment user interface is not terribly good. Some missed the submission date (although that wouldn't affect the review cycle as they wouldn't be in the pool) so it is reasonable to suspect that others will have misunderstood the review phase.

Perhaps the stage in the course also has an effect. The two points at which CS/programming courses tend to lose a lot of students are recursion and OOP. Given the nature of Java, it is impossible to avoid the latter but the Week 3/4 transition really marks the point at which the complexities of OOP start to become apparent. I wonder how many students submitted the Week 3 assessment, took a look at Week 4, decided it was just too difficult and walked away--leaving their peers unreviewed.

This failure is still, however, a puzzle and staff, far from being castigated for not making personal replies to individual posts, should be congratulated for acting quickly to extend the deadline.

I have just finished reviewing 48 assignments in an effort to help the system pull through (I've received my peer review results more than 7 days ago after having done my five). 

I should note that some submissions are so childish. Five out of 48 peers pasted in a word, a character or a one liner. If this correlates to a  substantial percentage percentage, then the system should be clogged with illegal submissions. 

I would suggest that edx or staff implement run a script to root these out and have those so called peers penalised for misuse of the review system.
Some students make dummy submissions simply so they can see the how others have tackled a problem (which may have defeated them).
ive seen things that looks pitiful. Some people dont even dare to try...

Dear learners,

We have created a self-assessment activity available [here][1] that is identical to the Peer Assessment of week 3. Those that have not received their grades need to follow these steps:

* Assess yourself using the scoring rubric you already know (also available as the second step of the activity)

We are giving you an entire week to complete this new self-assessment (deadline: 23 June, 11:59 am UTC). Please remember this is just for those learners who did the activity, but have not received their assessments.

As for peer assessment in week 5, you will still need to grade 5 peers, but you will only need to receive 3 peer assessments to complete the activity. This way we will make sure that all the learners who do the activity are finally assessed.

We apologize for the inconveniences this situation has caused.

Regards,

Java MOOC Team


  [1]: https://courses.edx.org/courses/UC3Mx/IT.1.1x/1T2015/courseware/17e592b2ab124b5eaeeebdfb3babc9e2/35b5dd7250cd4dd6b3fa7eed3634b1dd/

Dear learner,

Please find below the two prompts:

**PEER ASSESSMENT 1**

The dynamics between predators and preys in a given ecosystem attracts a lot of attention from researchers. Different scientists have developed Predator-Prey models to try to understand the interactions and foresee the evolution of the populations.

One of the first analyzed Predator-Prey ecosystems was the "Lynx - Snowshoe hare" and one of the first Predator-Prey models defined was the one developed by Lotka and Volterra.

This Predator-Prey model defines:

* H[n] as the snowshoe hare population (being n a specific moment of time)
* L[n] as the lynx population
* It assumes that the primary growth of the hare population in the absence of lynx is a1*H[n] and that the lynx population in the absence of hares declines -b1*L[n]
* It also assumes that the primary loss of snowshoe hares is due to predating -a2*H[n]*L[n] and the growth of the lynx population is from the energy derived from eating snowshoe hares b2*H[n]*L[n]

The Lotka-Volterra model is defined by the following formula:

H[n+1] = H[n] + a1 * H[n] - a2*H[n]*L[n] = H[n] * (1 + a1 - a2*L[n]) 
L[n+1] = L[n] - b1 * L[n] + b2*H[n]*L[n] = L[n] * (1 - b1 +b2*H[n])

For instance, let’s assume that the initial population of snowshoe hares is 300 and the initial population of lynxes is 20, and the values of the constants that regulate the model are a1 = 0.1, a2 = 0.01, b1 = 0.01 and b2 = 0.00002. The previous formula can be used to calculate the population of both lynxes and snowshoe hares after 2 periods:

After 1 period the population of snowshoe hares will be:
H[1] = H[0] + a1 * H[0] - a2*H[0]*L[0] = H[0] * (1 + a1 - a2*L[0]) = 300 *(1 + 0.1 - 0.01*20) = 270
In turn, the population of lynxes will be:
L[1] = L[0] - b1 * L[0] + b2*H[0]*L[0] = L[0] * (1 - b1 +b2*H[0]) = 20 * (1 - 0.01 + 0.00002*300) = 19.92

Notice that we keep the decimals for the following loop in the formula.

After 2 periods, the population of snowshoe hares will be:
H[2] = H

Can we have the staff’s opinion on this post : Correct reference code? What were we supposed to do exactly with the instruction "These access methods must check that only valid values are assigned; otherwise, an IllegalArgumentException with a descriptive message must be thrown." ?
Must these statements throws an exception or not :
(an administration employee must always have a temporary contract)
AdministrationEmployee al = new Administrationemployee(“Albert”);
al.setContract(Employee.INDEFINITE);
al.setDepartment("Management");

**"...clean way to prevent the user to put an incorrect value in a field..."**

Where would you verify the data? I would do it at the single point of entry where the value is being assigned to an attribute, the set member methods. If not done here, you will need to verify the data every time you call set member. That is a tedious and error prone approach. You can't guarantee others using your class will check value before calling set member.

**"We can’t accept that from the class 'Staff', you’ll put without problem the contract to TEMPORARY for an instance of the ManagementEmployee class, while instructions said it’s still INDEFINITE.
The way I've used is to *redefine (override) setContract in subclass ManagementEmployee (with a call to super.setContract())*.
I also verify if the number of years is greater than 0 in the Employee class and if it equals 0 in the AdministrationEmployee class, …
Is this the only way?"**

Your approach is convoluted. There is an easier and cleaner approach. However, the class hierarchy  and scope of accessors and mutators is flawed. All mutators should be private. There is no need to change there value after initial assignment, except for years.

This is solved by **not including** the contract value as a parameter to ManagementEmployee constructor. Then in the constructor for ManagementEmployee, hard code the contract value in the call to super.

**"On the other hand, always in a 'correct' OOP perspective, is it acceptable that the list of possible departments is defined in the generic class 'Employee' when it should depend either on the created subclasses, either on the Staff class (who 'represents' the company and 'knows' the departments that compose it) ?
If I add a new department (and create a new class of NewDepEmployee), I don't want to have to adapt the Employee class."**

Like I said earlier, this class hierarchy is flawed. You should not need a list of departments. The assignment should have used an abstract class Department a

Ok. Take a look at this project :
https://codeboard.io/projects/5510
And tell me where it is tedious and convoluted.

The basic controls, which matter for all employee (years must be positive, a name is composed by letters), take place in the Employee class ; the specific ones (a manager has an indefinite contract and an administrative, a temporary), in the subclasses. Even if the class hierarchy is flawed.
If you want to add a subclass, just add the subclass with its specific validation rules. If you want to remove one, just remove it.
Without any change at the Employee class.

Otherwise, why haven’t you written the getSalary directly in the Employee class ?
If(department==”Management”){
salary= 40000 + years * 6000
} else if(department==”Engineering”){...

We are talking apples and oranges. My comments are based on the flawed class hierarchy.

Employee shouldn't need to be subclassed. Its obvious name, contract, years and department are attributes of Employee. I still don't see the relevance of contract even though the problem states salary is dependent on it. It's not used in the salary calculation and only value is in implementation of toString.

If mutators are private, then the values are locked to when the object was instantiated. That is why I suggested all mutators except setYears by declared private. No additional verification of values needed beyond time of instantiation. Cleaner and smaller code.

 ***If mutators are public, it leaves the code open to unintended use.*** For example, your code for setDepartment only validates department. Not only does it throw a NullPointerException (code invokes equalsIgnoreCase without checking [null == department] first), it doesn't change how salary is now calculated. Even if you were to code the changes in getSalary, you will need to know all the calculations ahead of time. This is tedious and convoluted because you would have redundant code in getSalary like you suggested in your previous post.

Decoupling the relationship between Employee and Department will solve this. If employees are coupled to department, why is a mutator needed to change department?

The following code snippet shows how easy it is to define a new department and it's salary calculation. No redefinition of Employee required except department member is now an object instead of String.

title: ( n<m ) && ( n<=m )  means ( n < m ) , is this logically or mathematically true ?!!

children.body:
hi guys , 
in the first video of this section i determined it the professor talked about conjunction ( And gate ) 
and he says as example 
( n<m ) && ( n<=m )  means ( n < m ) ,
and i think this is logically and mathematically and even in program language ... Wrong !! 
so i wanted to make sure that i am right ... or i misunderstood the whole idea wrongly ??

I do admit having to think twice on this. 
It is both logically and mathematically true.
Using Venn diagram, you will notice that the overlap between the values (n<m) AND
 (n<=m) are the values (n<m).

I had to think twice on this too.
Using the Venn diagram, you will notice that the joint or overlap for the two sets is the area or values where n is less than m. For example:                          
A is the Set n Less Than m;  -∞,..,1,2,3,4,5,…,m-1
B is the Set n<=m;  -∞,..,1,2,3,4,5,…,m-1, m
The joint or overlap for Set A and B is the area or the values where n Less Than m.

title: Is the sorting method working right?

children.body:
I have some doubts about the second task here, that is, sorting an array.
First of all, what is 'int n' in the method declaration? void sort(int[] a, int n)
If we are using 'int n' in the for loop then we should know the exact number of times we should implement the for loop to traverse the array int[]a. Shouldn't we just use a.length-1 instead of 'int n'?

Next, the correct answer is a[0...k-1] <= a[k] for the while condition. But if it's true then we are just comparing two consequent numbers in the array and swap them if the preceding element in the array is less or equal to the following one. This doesn't sort the array at all.

Would be really nice to see actual example with real array implementing this method. 
Otherwise it's very confusing if we compare description and the method itself.

Could you explain why should n be equal to array.length-1? Due to the condition in  a for loop for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) we never access the n position in an array. That is, if we declare int n to be equal to array.length, it will work. And vice versa, if we declare int n to be equal to array.length-1, the last position in an array won't be checked and won't be sorted out.
N-1 is always the last position of the array and 0 the last. If you go backwards, the first will still be n-1. Using the n position will lead to an arrayindexoutofboundsexception

@ TanyaSilve

I was not attentive enough: You are right, in this exercise as it is given, n must be equal to length-of-array.    
But not because of the condition in the for loop that you mentioned:

Every (outer) iteration puts one element into the right place. You don't need an nth iteration for the last element. If n-1 elements are in place, the last one must be on its right place, too. 
To sort the array, the for loop only needs to run length-of-array - 1 times.    
 
Though, the condition of the while loop requires that n is equal to the length of the array: The maximum value for k must be length-of-array - 1. 

Thank you for pointing out this issue! 

To avoid the unnecessary iteration, I would change the condition of the while loop to   `(while (k != n+1))`   and choose n equal to length-of-array - 1

title: Cohesion of Methods. Magic Square Implementation

children.body:
"If you change these methods such as the first checks a square matrix being magic and the latter checks a magic square being normal"
- My logic was as follows (but you may well be correct): This leads to checkNormalMagicSquare being dependent on the implementation of checkMagicSquare() and an additional method call (getNormalMagicConstant and getMagicConstant instead of just getNormalMagicConstan).

"You are calling different methods for the different squares"
- This is deliberate. There may be cases where a user does not want the output related to normal magic squares.

In code design, there are always some tradeoffs that we have to handle. At the end, it's the programmer who decides...
The overall goal is to write code that is not only correct, but also to write code that is easily maintainable, by the programmer and other programmers. Therefore we have the two additional goals: Maximize maintainability and maximize readability. And I would add a third one, reusability. The checkNormalMagicSquare() would not be dependent on the implementation of checkMagicSquare. No matter what is the actual implementation of checkMagicSquare, checkNormalMagicSquare just calls the method and gets the result. This will still work if we apply changes to the checkMagicSquare method.
And that's exactly what we want to achieve: The independence of the actual implementation. The problem I see in your main method is not the call to different methods as such. Of course we (or any user of the program) might want to do different checks.
What I want to point to is the fact that *you call different methods to get the required output because you already know which magic square is normal and which is not*.
To see what I mean, you might want to change the names (only the names) of magicSquareJupiter and magicSquareSagradaFamilia and then decide if the result still matches what we wanted to achieve in this exercise. Please take my posts here not as a devaluation of your work - it's more the opposite.
When I stumbled over your code, I saw that your solution implements a lot of the principles we talked about in this course - in some parts very consequently, in others a bit less.
It's obvious that you reasoned a lot about the design, what should be done where etc. - so, take my posts as "fuel" for further reasoning ;)

Dear learner,

Once you are enrolled in a course the materials are available beyond the final date; the courses reach then a phase in which they are "archived". Courses, whether they are scheduled or self-paced, always reach a phase in which they are archived. 

Even when a course is archived you can actually access all the contents of this release, yet you will not be able to do the graded activities. The teaching staff do not monitor the course when this is archived.

A self-paced course is a different thing. This means that all the contents are available since the very first day of the course (instead of being opened week after week) and the due dates of the activities are flexibly scheduled (a single final due date instead of specific due dates for the weeks, for instance). During all the time the course is offered, the teaching staf is monitoring it.

Part 2 will be offered again soon. Remember what we did in Part 1: this was offered scheduled in April-June 2015, then self-paced in November 2015-June 2016.

Regards,

11111111=255 they asked for 127... so thats why it is not 11111111 and as for 4 bits 1110 is correct but for 8 bits 00001110 is a right answer. Thanks :)

Hello Arsal_99

01111111

actually it is very simple to transfer 8 bit binary into Hex and vice versa.

therefore 7F can be represented as 7 with 4 higher bits(0111)  and F with 4 lower bits(1111) - that what makes 01111111 (7F = 01111111). And as for translating to decimal - simply use formula (2 in power N) - 1, where N - is how many bits turned '1' counting from right to left. or simply add (2 in power 0)+(2 in power 1)+(2 in power 2)+...and so forth..(2 in power 6) = 127.

I hope that helped.

The formula is what you need to understand first: (C*9/5) +32 That becomes, (30*9/5) + 32 (30*1.8) +32 54 + 32 86 Final answer!!!

It is in the formula

multiple 30 and 1.8 to get 54. then add 54 and 32 to get 86 

1.8 is the same thing as 9/5

Good question Erikku_del_Rio. The formula is below:
The temperature T in degrees Fahrenheit (°F) is equal to the temperature T in degrees Celsius (°C) times 9/5 plus 32:

T(°F) = T(°C) × 9/5 + 32 

or

T(°F) = T(°C) × 1.8 + 32

correct answer of last one is 22. yo have to follow this code...first 4 times move forward then turn right and then 11th time move forward then turn right after that 5 time move forward

First remove the non-Java Symbols (like  ≤ ,=<) and the operand for two expressions (like 5 <= x <= 10), then the choices become easy except for 
a- 5 <= x || x <= 10 which is false
b- 5 <= x && x <= 10 which is True

a is false because it is an OR operation, so if any of the condition is true the statement is true, so if x=20 then the 5<=X will be true meaning the statement is true, and since the X must be between 5 and 10 then the choice is wrong.

b is True because it is an AND operation, so both of the conditions must be true for the statement to be true, and this make sure  the X can not be less than 5 or more than 10. 

Hope this help.

Thank you! This really helps. But I still have a question. What about
 !(x <5 || 10 < x) 
? I just couldn't figure it out myself, hope you could help me on this one. Thanks!

The OR condition is True IF any of its terms is True, ex: a|| b is true if:
a is true and b is false
b is true and b is false
a is true and b is true

the request is to have X to be between 5 and 10 including both, so its value should be one of the following:
5,6,7,8,9, or 10.

in case of x <5 || 10 < x this is true if 
i) x<5 is true 
or 
ii) 10<x is true
i --> give values to x=4,3,2,1,0,.....
ii --> give values to x= 11,12,13,........

if we combine i and ii , x value can be .....,0,1,2,3,4,11,12,13,14,.......... so it is False as x can only be 5,6,7,8,9,10

and !(False) is True.

children.body: You answered my next question yet :) , the garbage collector will delete the node to delete.

Uzair119,    
you are principally right: I will correct my statement above and replace the "can not" by "should not".      

Generally, if we avoid the use of references to null, our code is much clearer - and we avoid possible bugs.

Null values are troublesome and unsafe, so much so that we are well advised
to remove them from our design vocabulary. 

In most good Java programming — null values are *implicitly disallowed* in parameters and return values. 
That means, in good Java code, every method implicitly has a precondition on its parameters that they have to be non-null and  that its return value is non-null. 
If a method allows null values for a parameter, it should explicitly state it, or if it might return a null value as a result, it should explicitly state it.  
But it's always better to avoid null wherever we can.

To dive deeper into the subject, this [discussion][1] might be interesting...


  [1]: https://github.com/google/guava/wiki/UsingAndAvoidingNullExplained

title: Can I add a string and an int to get an int?
source_category: Week 1 / 1.2 Addition and Concatenation (non graded activity)

children.body: In the question three we have an int plus a string and we get an string, but is it possible to get a integer by adding strings?

> Java converts the the int to a String so it can concatenate them

The int is not converted to a string unless you tell Java to do so through either casting or a conversion function:

In the above example, 

int a = 2;
String b = "2";

a is a number
b is text

When you use '+' on more than one number, it's considered an addition operation:

a + a  returns 4

When you use '+' on more than one string, it combines them one after anohter:

b + b returns the text 22 (not a number but text)

Java can convert the "int" or the integer into a "string" .

  for exapmle if u have ;
 
 int n = 1; 
this  means that variable n contains the number "one" and its an integer.   And

string s = "1"
 and this means this is a text just like may be "hello" , so i f u are asked to get a certain string say 

string k = n+n;  this means you concatenate or add the value of the n which is 1+1= 2 since its an intgers and you add them  just the normal maths.

And if you are asked to get a certain string may be 

string m = s+n;   this means that you are supposed to concatenate a text and a number unlike in the case of adding two integers above so u will need to concatenate the text contained in string "s" which is '1' with the number in the int n which is also '1'. Therefore its going to be the "11" .  And thats the difference between them.

title: I did not get generate(123) is 321123..Please explain

children.body: I understand about recursion, stack, basecase...but still I did not get the login...Please help me understand correctly...

First, you've got 123. The left over of that, you know mate, is 3, cause it's with the integer 120, that 10 can be multiplied by another integer (12) to get close to the number, so you take 3. But we have something else there. It says you can't continue to the next and last order of the method ("System.out.print(x % 10);") till x/10==0. And till then, you do not scape form the loop. Why?, cause that {generate(x/10);} is between keys with no more orders.
   

 So the answer of 123/10 is 12. Straight you get the order of making the same computation with that 12. So 12/10=1.2, in integers, just 12/10=1, and the left over is 2, then you go to the deepest of the levels: 1/10=0.1, but in integers, is just 0, with a left over of 1. Being the answer 0, that means you can stop the loop, and follow the remaining orders, begining with the closest inner loop:  System.out.print(1 % 10)    // 1

You've gone out form the first if conditionant (generate(1)), for that inner method, you can fullfill now the last order: System.out.print(1 % 10)    // 1.

Notice you have alrready written for the moment= 3211.

and then you continue terminating the last order of the methods. How?, after you put that last 1, you would have unlocked the last step of the method generate(12), so,   System.out.print(12 % 10)       // 2

and then you got out of the 2 inner methods to get in the outter one and fullfill it's last order (step):  System.out.print(123 % 10)          // 3

321123

It took na while too. But I realized with this case in specific, that, within a method what it matters is the order of the steps, so. In case you get inside of a loop, you have to complete it to then, follow the next step. For example if it would have been = { System.out.print(x%10);
generate(x/10)} in the "if" loop, the answer would have been different and less cahothic= 332211
Great explanation!
very clear explanation thanks

title: Bloqueo de exámenes por exceder la fecha limite

children.body:
En mi opinión, los MOOC tienen la gran ventaja de la flexibilidad de horarios y de carga de trabajo cada semana. Marcar una fecha limite para los exámenes de los temas es contraproducente. creo que por el hecho de realizar todas las actividades de un tema, se debería permitir realizar el examen.

Saludos.

Hola,

la finalidad principal del curso es comenzar a aprender a programar y, para conseguirlo, no es necesario realizar las actividades con calificación ya que éstas solamente se completan para poder obtener el certificado verificado. 

Por otro lado, la primera vez que se lanza un MOOC en UC3M se utiliza una modalidad síncrona (la que ha habido en este MOOC) para poder atender de mejor manera los posibles problemas que puedan surgir. Después, cuando se vuelve a lanzar el curso en otro momento ya sí que se suele ofrecer de forma asíncrona (es decir, que cada uno aprende a su ritmo) y sin fechas de entrega.

También aprovecho para decir que este curso se va a ofrecer previsiblemente de nuevo en otoño por lo que, en ese momento, puedes volver a optar a realizar los exámenes.

Espero haberte resuelto todas las dudas.

Un saludo,

Nuria

Buenos días Nuria.

Te agradezco tu explicación, pero no entiendo que quieran realizar el curso en modalidad síncrona cuando se imparte por primera vez para realizar un mejor soporte al alumno. He hecho otros cursos en esta y en otras plataformas MOOC  en las que la fecha tope para examinarte de **todos** los temas era la misma, la de la finalización del curso, y te aseguro que se puede hacer un seguimiento muy exhaustivo por parte del profesor relajando las fechas hasta el final del curso, permitiendo así al alumno a conciliar su vida laboral y familiar con el curso. Supongo que no soy el único inscrito que tiene trabajo y la familia y que aprovecha los pocos tiempos muertos que quedan. A veces no se llegan a las fechas, y flexibilizarlas un poco puede ser la diferencia entre finalizar o abandonar el curso. 

Una duda ¿Cuando vuelvan a impartir el curso en otoño tendré que asistir desde cero o solo tengo que examinarme de los temas que me han faltado para completar el curso?

Muchas gracias por tu atención.

Opino igual que Apingut,

Muchas personas tienen trabajo, estudian una carrera, etc. a tiempo completo, y quieren aprovechar el poco tiempo que les queda para aprender algo nuevo. Sin embargo, las fechas limites para hacer los examenes impiden que los estudiantes tengan un buen aprendizaje, y la razón es que, muchas veces los temas no está bien claros, y se tiene que estudiar, investigar y practicar mucho para obtener una buena compresión del tema. 

El problema de eso, es que el tiempo se va muy rápido, y se atrasa mucho con los temas que corresponden de la semana, y cuando menos lo espera ya se abra terminado la fecha limite para realizar el examen.

Y solo queda dos opciones:
 1. Estudiar de manera rapida, sin tener una buena comprensión del tema, lo que ocasina un mal aprendizaje.
 2. Abandonar el curso.

Muchas gracias.

children.body:
En 6:47 se dice 

> "Si la primera expresión es true, verdadera, entonces se evalúa la segunda expresión."

En realidad, esto choca con la frase anterior, y debería decir 

> "Si la primera expresión es true, verdadera, entonces NO se evalúa la
> segunda expresión."

Puesto que es una OR, no una AND (en la AND, si el primer término es `false`, el resultado es `false`, valga lo que valga el segundo término, por lo que el segundo no se evaluará; en la OR, si el primer elemento es `true`, el resultado será `true`, y se comporta análogamente).

Un saludo.

Como cualquier operador, nos va a ser útil dependiendo lo que queramos hacer. Por ejemplo, si queremos saber si un número es divisible por 2, si el módulo es cero sabemos que es divisible: 4%2=0 pues 4 es divisible por 2 por el contrario 5%2=1 pues 5 no es divisible por 2.
esta facil

A primera vista parece que este operador no va a ser muy utilizado en programación, pero resuelve infinidad de problemas, un ejemplo básico seria en saber si un número es par o impar, los números pares si los dividimos entre 2 siempre son exactos, por tanto, el resto es cero y para los impares tendrá un resto diferente de cero, con el uso de este operador módulo se resolvería el problema. Otro ejemplo seria la devolución del cambio en una máquina expendedora de algún producto x. Saludos

title: What is the situation in other regions like Africa, Asia?
source_category: Week 1 / 1.a.2. The rise of learning analytics in higher education - Part 2

children.body:
It is wonderful to see that learning analytics are getting the spotlight in some countries, but I think that other countries already have some sort of systems to apply Learning Analytics in some way. For example, In my country Morocco, we have a unique system to collect students data in one place, in order to standerise and analyse what is going on in different regions, and from there cross reference the data with other data, in order to make educational policies. But, I think personally that the lack of skillful Educational Data analytics specialists is what makes LA impact less noticable in my country.
My question to the community is : Do you think that learning analytics already exists in most educational systems in the world?
Perhaps this type of data collection is better termed 'academic analytics' as it is more focused on administrative (funding, and perhaps student support) issues? What do others think?

Thank you, that is very interesting and helpful. Yes, I think here in the US at least we have resources (time and money) dedicated to identifying institutional information like time-to-degree and persistence, and quite a bit less dedicated to supporting students (e.g., early alert systems referring students for tutoring). Nevertheless, there is still quite a bit of interest in what is available, how it works, and how it supports the learning by also supporting the function of teaching.

Hello. I think there has been a lot of research progress in LA in the last years. But in most institutions LA has not been adopted in a large scale. 

There are projects to build capacities in LA such as the LALA project that aims to build capacities to use LA in Latin American:

https://www.lalaproject.org

Cohesión es un sinónimo de Acoplamiento.
Una baja cohesión en un programa no puede conseguirse sin un alto nivel de acoplamiento.
Una alta cohesión normalmente está correlado con un bajo acoplamiento.
La cohesión es un concepto que solo es aplicable a nivel de método.
La cohesión se aplica a métodos, pero también a clases.
El acoplamiento se refiere al grado en el que lo elementos de un módulo tienen relación.
El acoplamiento se refiere al grado de independencia entre módulos software.
Una manera de conseguir cohesión de métodos es forzar a un método responsabilizarse de una única tarea muy bien definida
la utilización de encapsulación e interfaces no ayudan cuando intentamos reducir el nivel de acoplamiento
Usar variables estáticas puede llevar a un acoplamiento implícito

title: Can u Explain last multiple choice

children.body:
Which of the following algorithms on arrays is typically less computationally expensive?
why getting information of last portion is different than first portion.

Since you can easily get the position of the last element which is static, so it is kind of faster. To insert new information to the FIRST EMPTY POSITION you would have to iterate, so that u can find it, which will take more time. Hope this help!!!

Because you already have a fixed length of the array. You already know what the last index will be. So if you find the last index, you basically just "jump" to that area and boom you got the index. Instead of going through each and every index from the beginning and checking if its empty or not.

When we retrieve the last index we are going directly to that index and getting the info there. This actually doesn't require a search through the array, as we are targeting a specific index in the array. The time complexity for this is O(1) aka constant. Meanwhile, the search for the first empty index requires that we scroll through the array until we find an empty index. Worst case, this can mean going to the end of the array, i.e. the time complexity for that is O(n) aka linear.

Hola: 
¿Puede alguien ayudarme con esta respuesta no entiendo dónde está el fallo?
Respuesta 5 <= x || x <= 10

Gracias por adelantado
Gracias por la explicación, por fin he entendido qué es lo que estaba pasando por alto.
El enunciado dice expresamente que X esté entre 5 y 10 **ambos inclusive**, lo que significa que el 5 y el 10 también están en el intervalo.

En la primera expresión tienes: 4 < x && !(x > 10) que podemos leer como "x es mayor (no mayor o igual) que 4 y x **NO** (por la negación !) es mayor que 10. "x es mayor que 4" es lo mismo que "x es mayor o igual a 5" y "x no es mayor que 10" es lo mismo que "x es menor o igual a 10". Como el operador del medio es: &&, se tienen que cumplir ambas a la vez: x mayor o igual a 5 **Y** menor o igual a 10.

En la segunda expresión: !(x < 5 || 10 < x) tenemos la parte entre paréntesis: "x es menor que 5 **O** x es mayor que 10" que es justo lo contrario a lo que queremos. Como después tiene una negación fuera, entonces ya tenemos lo que queremos.

Si no lo ves así, dibuja una tabla y pon 5 columnas: col1(X), col2(X < 5), col3(10 < X), col4(col2 || col3), col5(!col4). El resultado final es la col5. Dale 3 valores a la col1: un número menor que 5, otro entre 5 y 10 y otro mayor que 10; (puedes dar también el 5 y el 10 para ver cómo se comporta en los extremos) y vas completando las otras columnas con sí o no (verdadero/false, true/false, 1/0, ... como más te guste). Al final verás como la col5 tiene: verdadero, true o 1 sólo en las filas en las que la col1 tiene un número entre 5 y 10, ambos incluidos.

title: Anyone else still working on this class?

children.body: Is anyone still working through this course? I am trying to keep up with this course while going to college so I a bit behind, but I still do not understand  what I am learning and it definitely makes me feel I just too stupid to learn how to code. I feel very deflated realizing I cannot grasp this stuff online, but I also do not have $10,00 0 to pay for a bootcamp to have someone teach me in person.

I  also couldn't pass my first exam. I got stuck on Blocky on the count down turns as I run out of blocks before it could finish the maze. I don't understand what the blocks are doing and if writing code, I don't think it would be that difficult. Since I couldn't figure it out, I didn't even bother with the rest of the mazes. I am not going to take exam2. I am going to just go on and hope maybe by the end of the course I can understand it enough to go back and take the exam. I assume since I failed the first test I will not earn my certificate anyway at this point. 

Maybe if we just work through the course it will come together by the end. 

I have watched the videos a few times each trying to grasp the concepts.

Let's just think positive that we can do it!
Yeah agree. dont worry I meant i failed in my college java exam. not here. since I still haven't done whole course yet here. Good luck mate ;)
Sorry to hear about your exam. Good luck in this course.
